---
title: "CCP_Modul_8_CtoC_Profile"
author: "izb"
format: html
editor: visual
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: true
  message: false
  error: true
editor: source
---

# MODUL 8 — Coherence-to-Cohesion Profile

MODUL 8 consolidates the final frozen indices from the preceding modules into indices bundles for the four target texts. Each indices bundle is presented in a sparse table before exactly corresponding LM and Human measures are compared.

The module preserves the original measures, their textual levels, and their methodological origins. It does not mix measures from different indices bundles. Human-referenced differences are calculated only between directly corresponding measures.

To complete the Coherence-to-Cohesion profile construction is at this point prioritized over any future scalar representation (which was considered).

# MODUL 8.1 — Initialize the CtoC profile workflow

```{r}
# MODUL 8.1 — Initialize the CtoC profile workflow

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tibble)
library(gt)

project_root <- normalizePath("..", winslash = "/", mustWork = TRUE)
measure_output_dir <- file.path(project_root, "measure_outputs")
metadata_dir <- file.path(project_root, "metadata")
modul_8_output_dir <- file.path(measure_output_dir, "modul_8_c2c_profile")

dir.create(modul_8_output_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

stopifnot(
  dir.exists(project_root),
  dir.exists(measure_output_dir),
  dir.exists(metadata_dir),
  dir.exists(modul_8_output_dir)
)

modul_8_paths <- list(
  input_inventory = file.path(modul_8_output_dir, "modul_8_input_inventory.rds"),
  canonical_text_register = file.path(modul_8_output_dir, "modul_8_canonical_text_register.rds"),
  component_registry = file.path(modul_8_output_dir, "modul_8_component_registry.rds"),
  c2c_profile = file.path(modul_8_output_dir, "modul_8_c2c_profile.rds"),
  human_referenced_differences = file.path(modul_8_output_dir, "modul_8_human_referenced_differences.rds"),
  final_audit = file.path(modul_8_output_dir, "modul_8_final_audit.rds")
)

modul_8_indices_bundles <- c(
  "neurolinguistical",
  "psycholinguistic",
  "POS_and_function_word",
  "referential_cohesion",
  "lexical_cohesion",
  "semantic_continuity",
  "prompt_and_whole_text_similarity",
  "distributional_plausibility"
)

modul_8_registry_fields <- c(
  "indices_bundle",
  "measure",
  "interpretative_frame",
  "text_chunk",
  "primary_function",
  "secondary_function",
  "source_module",
  "enters_D"
)

modul_8_allowed_interpretative_frames <- c(
  "narrow_coherence",
  "cc_interface",
  "narrow_cohesion",
  "not_sure"
)

modul_8_allowed_text_chunks <- c("micro", "meso", "macro", "not_sure")
modul_8_allowed_enters_D <- c("yes", "no")

modul_8_presentation_rules <- tibble::tribble(
  ~value_type,        ~presentation_rule,
  "count",            "integer",
  "PMI",              "integer",
  "other_continuous", "three_decimal_places",
  "missing",          "NA"
)

stopifnot(
  length(modul_8_indices_bundles) == 8L,
  identical(
    modul_8_registry_fields,
    c(
      "indices_bundle", "measure", "interpretative_frame", "text_chunk",
      "primary_function", "secondary_function", "source_module", "enters_D"
    )
  )
)

cat("MODUL 8 CtoC profile workflow initialized.\n")
cat("Project root:", project_root, "\n")
cat("Indices bundles:", length(modul_8_indices_bundles), "\n")
cat("Output directory:", modul_8_output_dir, "\n")
```

# MODUL 8.2 — Define and freeze the canonical text register

This cell defines the four canonical target texts, records the exact LLM specifications, identifies the Human reference, and freezes the common text order used throughout MODUL 8.

```{r}

# MODUL 8.2 — Define and freeze the canonical text register

canonical_text_register <- tibble::tribble(
  ~text_id,                   ~text_label, ~text_order, ~source_type, ~provider,  ~model_specification, ~is_human_reference,
  "HUMAN_BASELINE_001",       "Human",              1L, "human",      NA,         NA,                   TRUE,
  "BASIC_CHATGPT_NATIVE_001", "ChatGPT",            2L, "LM",         "OpenAI",   "gpt-4o-mini",        FALSE,
  "BASIC_GEMINI_NATIVE_001",  "Gemini",             3L, "LM",         "Google",   "gemini-2.5-flash",   FALSE,
  "BASIC_CLAUDE_NATIVE_001",  "Claude",             4L, "LM",         "Anthropic","claude-haiku-4.5",   FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    text_label = factor(text_label, levels = c("Human", "ChatGPT", "Gemini", "Claude"))
  )

stopifnot(
  nrow(canonical_text_register) == 4L,
  !anyDuplicated(canonical_text_register$text_id),
  sum(canonical_text_register$is_human_reference) == 1L,
  identical(
    canonical_text_register$text_id[canonical_text_register$is_human_reference],
    "HUMAN_BASELINE_001"
  ),
  all(!is.na(
    canonical_text_register$model_specification[
      canonical_text_register$source_type == "LM"
    ]
  ))
)

saveRDS(canonical_text_register, modul_8_paths$canonical_text_register)
canonical_text_register <- readRDS(modul_8_paths$canonical_text_register)

print(canonical_text_register, n = Inf)
cat("Canonical MODUL 8 text register frozen and reloaded.\n")
```

# MODUL 8.3 — Calculate and freeze prompt-length diagnostics

This cell creates the first independent length-diagnostics table by recording compliance with the prompted word length; further frozen sentence-, token-, and frequency-based length measures will be added after their exact source objects have been audited.

# MODUL 8.3 — Calculate and freeze prompt-length diagnostics

```{r}

# MODUL 8.3 — Calculate and freeze prompt-length diagnostics

modul_8_length_diagnostics_path <- file.path(
  modul_8_output_dir,
  "modul_8_length_diagnostics.rds"
)

length_diagnostics <- canonical_text_register |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    n_word_tokens = dplyr::case_when(
      text_id == "BASIC_CHATGPT_NATIVE_001" ~ 439L,
      text_id == "BASIC_GEMINI_NATIVE_001" ~ 458L,
      text_id == "BASIC_CLAUDE_NATIVE_001" ~ 324L,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    ),
    prompt_word_limit = 549L,
    prompt_length_proportion = n_word_tokens / prompt_word_limit,
    prompt_length_shortfall = prompt_word_limit - n_word_tokens
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(
    text_id,
    text_label,
    n_word_tokens,
    prompt_word_limit,
    prompt_length_proportion,
    prompt_length_shortfall
  )

stopifnot(
  nrow(length_diagnostics) == 4L,
  !anyDuplicated(length_diagnostics$text_id),
  all(
    length_diagnostics$n_word_tokens[
      length_diagnostics$text_id != "HUMAN_BASELINE_001"
    ] > 0L
  )
)

saveRDS(length_diagnostics, modul_8_length_diagnostics_path)
length_diagnostics <- readRDS(modul_8_length_diagnostics_path)

length_diagnostics_print <- length_diagnostics |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    prompt_length_proportion = sprintf("%.3f", prompt_length_proportion)
  )

print(length_diagnostics_print, n = Inf)
cat("Prompt-length diagnostics frozen and reloaded.\n")
```

# MODUL 8.4 — Create the lexical-cohesion indices-bundle table

```{r}
# MODUL 8.4 — Create the lexical-cohesion indices-bundle table

ccp_module_5c_y4 <- readRDS(
  file.path(measure_output_dir, "ccp_module_5c_y4.rds")
)

modul_8_lexical_cohesion <- canonical_text_register |>
  dplyr::select(text_id, text_label, text_order) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    ccp_module_5c_y4 |>
      dplyr::select(
        text_id,
        n_paragraphs,
        n_adjacent_paragraph_pairs,
        y4_all_lemma_overlap_mean,
        y4_content_overlap_mean,
        noun_transition_sum,
        pronoun_transition_sum,
        adverb_transition_sum,
        potential_nominal_reference_transition_sum,
        noun_to_potential_pronoun_ratio
      ),
    by = "text_id"
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(text_order)

stopifnot(
  nrow(modul_8_lexical_cohesion) == 4L,
  !anyDuplicated(modul_8_lexical_cohesion$text_id),
  all(!is.na(modul_8_lexical_cohesion$n_paragraphs))
)

modul_8_lexical_cohesion_path <- file.path(
  modul_8_output_dir,
  "modul_8_lexical_cohesion.rds"
)

saveRDS(
  modul_8_lexical_cohesion,
  modul_8_lexical_cohesion_path
)

modul_8_lexical_cohesion <- readRDS(
  modul_8_lexical_cohesion_path
)

modul_8_lexical_cohesion_print <- modul_8_lexical_cohesion |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(
      c(
        y4_all_lemma_overlap_mean,
        y4_content_overlap_mean,
        noun_to_potential_pronoun_ratio
      ),
      ~ ifelse(is.na(.x), NA_character_, sprintf("%.3f", .x))
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(-text_order)

print(
  modul_8_lexical_cohesion_print,
  n = Inf,
  width = Inf
)

cat("MODUL 8 lexical-cohesion table frozen and reloaded.\n")
```

## Note — Measures in the lexical-cohesion indices bundle

The lexical-cohesion indices bundle summarizes how lexical material and potential referential forms are carried across adjacent paragraph boundaries. All transition-based measures are calculated over consecutive paragraph pairs within each target text.

-   **`n_paragraphs`**: total number of body-text paragraphs in the target text.

-   **`n_adjacent_paragraph_pairs`**: number of consecutive paragraph transitions available for analysis. For a text with (p) paragraphs, the maximum number of adjacent pairs is (p-1).

-   **`y4_all_lemma_overlap_mean`**: mean Jaccard overlap between the complete lemma sets of adjacent paragraphs. Higher values indicate that consecutive paragraphs reuse a larger proportion of their lexical material.

-   **`y4_content_overlap_mean`**: mean Jaccard overlap between the content-word lemma sets of adjacent paragraphs. It focuses on lexical-semantic continuity by excluding function words from the overlap calculation.

-   **`noun_transition_sum`**: total number of noun lemmas transferred across adjacent paragraph boundaries, meaning that the same noun lemma occurs in both paragraphs of a transition.

-   **`pronoun_transition_sum`**: total number of pronoun forms transferred across adjacent paragraph boundaries. This measure records repeated pronominal material but does not by itself establish coreference.

-   **`adverb_transition_sum`**: total number of adverb lemmas transferred across adjacent paragraph boundaries. Recurrent adverbs may contribute to temporal, spatial, causal, or discourse-organizational continuity.

-   **`potential_nominal_reference_transition_sum`**: total number of transitions in which nominal material from one paragraph may be continued through a potential referring form in the adjacent paragraph. This is a structural referential-cohesion diagnostic rather than a resolved coreference measure.

-   **`noun_to_potential_pronoun_ratio`**: ratio between transferred noun material and potential pronominal continuation across paragraph transitions. It describes the relative balance between lexical repetition and possible pronominal reference maintenance.

The two Jaccard measures are continuous similarity values, whereas the transition sums are integer counts. These measures remain separate in MODUL 8 and are not numerically combined into a single lexical-cohesion score.

# REFERENCE: \*Straka, Milan, Jan Hajič, and Jana Straková. 2016. “UDPipe: Trainable Pipeline for Processing CoNLL-U Files.” In Proceedings of LREC 2016.

Note on y4 indices computed in modul 5: In MODUL 5, the construction of (y_4) concerned adjacent-paragraph lemma overlap. It was parser- and POS-based and used the German model available through the R package `udpipe`, which provides tokenization, lemmatization, POS tagging and dependency parsing.

After parsing, the annotated lemmas were reconnected to the paragraph metadata, creating the paragraph-level annotation layer. The underlying layer is token-based because each row represents a token occurrence, but the (y_4) overlap measure itself is lemma-based because lemma values are compared across paragraphs.

For this purpose, adjacent paragraph pairs were created, such as paragraph 1–paragraph 2, paragraph 2–paragraph 3 and paragraph 3–paragraph 4. Pairwise overlap was calculated and then summarized by text.

A separate content-word lemma layer was also created. Adjacent content-word overlap was computed, and lemmas shared by adjacent paragraphs were identified.

Transferred lemmas were additionally counted by category for each paragraph transition. This included shared content words, pronouns, adverbs and other retained categories.

The number of adjacent transitions indicates across how many paragraph boundaries a lemma was transferred. For example, if *Arbeit* occurs across three transitions, it is shared across four consecutive paragraphs. *Digitalisierung* may occur across two transitions and *verändern* across one.

Lexical overlap represents overt semantic continuity, although high overlap may also indicate redundancy. The noun, pronoun and adverb diagnostics were included to examine whether cohesion was more content-word-driven or pronoun-driven.

These measures concern potential antecedent–anaphor relations, but they do not resolve actual anaphoric reference. They also cannot directly determine given–new structure, although repeated lemmas may contribute to informationally given material and less frequent lemmas to informational development.

The final (y_4) profile contains the adjacent-paragraph overlap measures, the category-specific transfer counts and the (y_4) decay plot. The plot shows how lexical overlap changes across successive paragraph transitions.

# MODUL 8.5 — Format the lexical-cohesion indices-bundle table

```{r}
# MODUL 8.5 — Format the lexical-cohesion indices-bundle table

modul_8_lexical_cohesion_gt <- modul_8_lexical_cohesion |>
  dplyr::select(
    text_label,
    n_paragraphs,
    n_adjacent_paragraph_pairs,
    y4_all_lemma_overlap_mean,
    y4_content_overlap_mean,
    noun_transition_sum,
    pronoun_transition_sum,
    adverb_transition_sum,
    potential_nominal_reference_transition_sum,
    noun_to_potential_pronoun_ratio
  ) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "text_label") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**Lexical-cohesion indices bundle: adjacent-paragraph lemma overlap**"),
    subtitle = gt::md("$y_4$ ($\\varepsilon_4$), summarized by target text")
  ) |>
  gt::cols_label(
    n_paragraphs = gt::md("Paragraphs"),
    n_adjacent_paragraph_pairs = gt::md("Adjacent<br>pairs"),
    y4_all_lemma_overlap_mean = gt::md("All-lemma<br>overlap"),
    y4_content_overlap_mean = gt::md("Content-word<br>overlap"),
    noun_transition_sum = gt::md("Noun<br>transfers"),
    pronoun_transition_sum = gt::md("Pronoun<br>transfers"),
    adverb_transition_sum = gt::md("Adverb<br>transfers"),
    potential_nominal_reference_transition_sum = gt::md("Potential nominal<br>reference"),
    noun_to_potential_pronoun_ratio = gt::md("Noun–pronoun<br>ratio")
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(
    columns = c(
      n_paragraphs,
      n_adjacent_paragraph_pairs,
      noun_transition_sum,
      pronoun_transition_sum,
      adverb_transition_sum,
      potential_nominal_reference_transition_sum
    )
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(
      y4_all_lemma_overlap_mean,
      y4_content_overlap_mean,
      noun_to_potential_pronoun_ratio
    ),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  gt::sub_missing(missing_text = "NA") |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "center", columns = gt::everything()) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = "#000000"),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_title(groups = c("title", "subtitle")),
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_stub(),
      gt::cells_body()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "all",
      color = "#000000",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_stub(),
      gt::cells_body()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.width = gt::pct(100),
    table.font.size = gt::px(11),
    heading.title.font.size = gt::px(14),
    heading.subtitle.font.size = gt::px(11),
    data_row.padding = gt::px(4),
    column_labels.padding = gt::px(5),
    row.striping.include_table_body = FALSE,
    table.background.color = "#FFFFFF",
    heading.background.color = "#FFFFFF",
    column_labels.background.color = "#FFFFFF",
    stub.background.color = "#FFFFFF",
    table.border.top.style = "solid",
    table.border.top.width = gt::px(1),
    table.border.top.color = "#000000",
    table.border.bottom.style = "solid",
    table.border.bottom.width = gt::px(1),
    table.border.bottom.color = "#000000",
    heading.border.bottom.style = "solid",
    heading.border.bottom.width = gt::px(1),
    heading.border.bottom.color = "#000000"
  )

modul_8_lexical_cohesion_gt
```

# exploration for future

```{r}
# MODUL 8.6 — Plot the y4 lemma-transfer heat map
```

# MODUL 6 Indices BUNDELS

### MODUL 6 — LSA-based semantic-continuity indices

The first part of the MODUL 6 semantic-continuity analysis calculated (x3s), adjacent-sentence cosine similarity, and (x3p), adjacent-paragraph cosine similarity. The sentence and paragraph segments had already been folded into the frozen actual-LSA space and L2-normalized. Because the vectors had unit length, the cosine similarity between two adjacent segments could be calculated as their dot product.

For (x3s), every sentence vector was compared with the vector of the following sentence. For (x3p), every paragraph vector was compared with the vector of the following paragraph. The pair-level values were then summarized separately for each target text.

The resulting text-level (x3) profile contained the mean and standard deviation of adjacent-sentence similarity, the mean adjacent-paragraph similarity, and the respective numbers of available adjacent pairs. These summaries represented the general semantic-continuity level of each text, but sentence-level and paragraph-level continuity remained separate indices.

The second part calculated the within-text (x3s) semantic-continuity trajectory. The positions of the sentence pairs were normalized to a common scale from 0 at the beginning of the text to 1 at the end. A separate linear regression of adjacent-sentence cosine similarity on normalized position was then fitted for each text.

The sentence-level trajectory diagnostics contained the slope, its standard error, (R\^2), the mean similarity in the first, middle and final thirds of the text, and retention, calculated as the final-third mean minus the first-third mean.

A positive slope indicates that adjacent-sentence semantic similarity tends to increase as the text progresses. A negative slope indicates decreasing semantic continuity, while a slope close to zero indicates a comparatively stable trajectory. The (R\^2) value indicates how strongly the observed sentence-pair similarities follow a linear development and must not be interpreted as the level of semantic continuity itself.

Retention compares the final and first thirds directly. A positive retention value indicates higher adjacent-sentence similarity in the final third than in the first third, whereas a negative value indicates lower final-third similarity.

The sentence-gradient plot displays the observed adjacent-sentence cosine similarities across normalized sentence-pair position and adds one fitted linear trajectory per text. The labels report the slope, standard error, (R\^2), first-, middle- and final-third means, and retention.

# \# MODUL 8.7 — Load the core x3 semantic-continuity indices

This cell loads the frozen canonical x3s and x3p outputs and creates one text-level table containing their continuity levels and available pair counts. It does not recalculate LSA vectors, cosine similarities, or within-text gradients.

# MODUL 8.7 — Load the core x3 semantic-continuity indices

```{r}

# MODUL 8.7 — Load the core x3 semantic-continuity indices

x3s_path <- file.path(
  measure_output_dir,
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x3s_sentence_continuity.rds"
)

x3p_path <- file.path(
  measure_output_dir,
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x3p_paragraph_continuity.rds"
)

stopifnot(
  file.exists(x3s_path),
  file.exists(x3p_path)
)

x3s_bundle <- readRDS(x3s_path)
x3p_bundle <- readRDS(x3p_path)

modul_8_x3_core <- canonical_text_register |>
  dplyr::select(
    text_id,
    text_label,
    text_order
  ) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    x3s_bundle$profiles |>
      dplyr::select(
        text_id,
        x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_mean,
        x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_sd,
        n_x3s_adjacent_sentence_pairs
      ),
    by = "text_id"
  ) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    x3p_bundle$profiles |>
      dplyr::select(
        text_id,
        x3p_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean,
        n_x3p_adjacent_paragraph_pairs
      ),
    by = "text_id"
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(text_order)

stopifnot(
  nrow(modul_8_x3_core) == 4L,
  !anyDuplicated(modul_8_x3_core$text_id),
  all(!is.na(
    modul_8_x3_core$
      x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_mean
  )),
  all(!is.na(
    modul_8_x3_core$
      x3p_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean
  ))
)

print(
  modul_8_x3_core,
  n = Inf,
  width = Inf
)
```

# MODUL 8.7a — Display the core x3 semantic-continuity indices

```{r}

# MODUL 8.7a — Display the core x3 semantic-continuity indices

modul_8_x3_core_gt <- modul_8_x3_core |>
  dplyr::select(
    text_label,
    n_x3s_adjacent_sentence_pairs,
    x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_mean,
    x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_sd,
    n_x3p_adjacent_paragraph_pairs,
    x3p_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean
  ) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "text_label") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**x3 semantic-continuity indices**"),
    subtitle = "Adjacent-sentence and adjacent-paragraph cosine-similarity profiles by target text"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Adjacent sentences (x3s)",
    columns = c(
      n_x3s_adjacent_sentence_pairs,
      x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_mean,
      x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_sd
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Adjacent paragraphs (x3p)",
    columns = c(
      n_x3p_adjacent_paragraph_pairs,
      x3p_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean
    )
  ) |>
  gt::cols_label(
    n_x3s_adjacent_sentence_pairs = "Pairs",
    x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_mean = "Mean",
    x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_sd = "SD",
    n_x3p_adjacent_paragraph_pairs = "Pairs",
    x3p_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean = "Mean"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(
    columns = c(
      n_x3s_adjacent_sentence_pairs,
      n_x3p_adjacent_paragraph_pairs
    ),
    use_seps = FALSE
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(
      x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_mean,
      x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_sd,
      x3p_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean
    ),
    decimals = 3,
    use_seps = FALSE
  ) |>
  gt::sub_missing(
    columns = gt::everything(),
    missing_text = "NA"
  ) |>
  gt::cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = gt::everything()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = paste(
      "x3s is the mean cosine similarity between adjacent sentence vectors;",
      "x3p is the mean cosine similarity between adjacent paragraph vectors.",
      "The two levels remain separate indices."
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    table.font.color = "black",
    table.border.top.color = "black",
    table.border.top.width = gt::px(1),
    table.border.bottom.color = "black",
    table.border.bottom.width = gt::px(1),
    heading.background.color = "white",
    heading.border.bottom.color = "black",
    heading.border.bottom.width = gt::px(1),
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    column_labels.border.top.color = "black",
    column_labels.border.top.width = gt::px(1),
    column_labels.border.bottom.color = "black",
    column_labels.border.bottom.width = gt::px(1),
    stub.background.color = "white",
    stub.font.weight = "bold",
    stub.border.color = "black",
    stub.border.width = gt::px(1),
    data_row.padding = gt::px(5),
    source_notes.background.color = "white"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "all",
      color = "black",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_column_spanners(),
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_stub(),
      gt::cells_body()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "top",
      color = "black",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = gt::cells_source_notes()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(
      color = "black",
      weight = "bold"
    ),
    locations = gt::cells_column_spanners()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = "black"),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_title(groups = "title"),
      gt::cells_title(groups = "subtitle"),
      gt::cells_body(),
      gt::cells_stub(),
      gt::cells_source_notes()
    )
  )

modul_8_x3_core_gt
```
# gt::tab_source_note(
  source_note = paste(
    "Measure: cosine similarity between adjacent L2-normalized LSA vectors.",
    "x3s reports sentence-level transitions; x3p reports paragraph-level transitions.",
    "Means and SD are macro-level text observations derived from those transition cosines."
  )
)

# Note: Core x3 semantic-continuity indices

x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_mean is the mean cosine similarity between consecutive sentence vectors and represents the general local semantic-continuity level of each text.

x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity_sd describes the variability of the adjacent-sentence similarities within the text.

x3p_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean is the mean cosine similarity between consecutive paragraph vectors and represents the general paragraph-level semantic-continuity level.

The sentence and paragraph means remain separate indices. They are not averaged into one combined x3 score.

# MODUL 8.8 — Add the x3s within-text trajectory diagnostics

This cell loads the frozen x3s gradient diagnostics and joins them to the core x3 text-level table. It does not refit the within-text regression or replace the mean continuity levels with trajectory measures.

```{r}

# MODUL 8.8 — Add the x3s within-text trajectory diagnostics

x3s_gradient_path <- file.path(
  measure_output_dir,
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x3_gradient.rds"
)

stopifnot(
  file.exists(x3s_gradient_path)
)

x3s_gradient_bundle <- readRDS(
  x3s_gradient_path
)

x3s_gradient_diagnostics <-
  x3s_gradient_bundle$diagnostics

modul_8_x3_core_extended <- modul_8_x3_core |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    x3s_gradient_diagnostics |>
      dplyr::select(
        text_id,
        x3_adjacent_sentence_similarity_gradient,
        x3_adjacent_sentence_gradient_se,
        x3_adjacent_sentence_gradient_r2,
        x3_adjacent_sentence_similarity_first_third,
        x3_adjacent_sentence_similarity_middle_third,
        x3_adjacent_sentence_similarity_final_third,
        x3_adjacent_sentence_similarity_retention
      ),
    by = "text_id"
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(text_order)

stopifnot(
  nrow(modul_8_x3_core_extended) == 4L,
  !anyDuplicated(
    modul_8_x3_core_extended$text_id
  ),
  all(!is.na(
    modul_8_x3_core_extended$
      x3_adjacent_sentence_similarity_gradient
  ))
)

print(
  modul_8_x3_core_extended,
  n = Inf,
  width = Inf
)
```

Note — Additional within-text x3 gradient

A new directional index was explored as an analogue to the retention/decay logic used for y3d.

x3_adjacent_sentence_similarity_gradient

x3_adjacent_sentence_similarity_retention

For each text, the adjacent-sentence pairs were ordered as:

S1↔S2 S2↔S3 S3↔S4 and so forth.

Each pair already had one cosine-similarity value. Because the texts contain different numbers of sentence pairs, pair position was converted to a common scale:

pi= i−1/ n−1 where 0 represents the first sentence boundary and 1 represents the final sentence boundary.

A separate linear regression was then fitted within each text:

si=β0 + β1 pi+εi The slope β1 is the proposed gradient.

The model asks:

As we move from the beginning toward the end of this text, do the observed adjacent-sentence cosine values generally increase, decrease, or remain stable? It does not predict semantic continuity from linguistic features. It estimates whether the already observed adjacent-sentence cosine similarities exhibit an overall linear increase or decrease across normalized text position. It is just a directional descriptor within the x3 semantic-continuity indices.

A positive slope indicates that adjacent-sentence similarity increases across the text. A slope near zero indicates no strong directional change, while a negative slope indicates decreasing adjacent-sentence similarity.

The value represents the estimated change from normalized position 0 to normalized position 1. It does not measure the absolute level of continuity. A text can have a low mean and a stable slope, or a high mean and a strongly negative slope. Therefore, the gradient must be reported together with the mean. 

# MODUL 8.9 — Display the Human-referenced x3 trajectory plot

```{r}

# MODUL 8.9 — Print-friendly Human-referenced x3 trajectory plot

x3_human_reference_path <- file.path(
  measure_output_dir,
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x3_human_reference.rds"
)

stopifnot(file.exists(x3_human_reference_path))

x3_human_predictions <- readRDS(
  x3_human_reference_path
)$predictions |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    text_label = dplyr::recode(
      as.character(model),
      human = "Human",
      chatgpt_native = "ChatGPT",
      gemini_native = "Gemini",
      claude_native = "Claude"
    ),
    text_label = factor(
      text_label,
      levels = c("Human", "ChatGPT", "Gemini", "Claude")
    )
  )

x3_line_labels <- x3_human_predictions |>
  dplyr::group_by(text_label) |>
  dplyr::slice_min(
    abs(normalized_position - 0.5),
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

modul_8_x3_human_reference_plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  x3_human_predictions,
  ggplot2::aes(
    x = normalized_position,
    y = predicted_similarity,
    group = text_label,
    linetype = text_label
  )
) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(
    colour = "black",
    linewidth = 0.35
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_label(
    data = x3_line_labels,
    ggplot2::aes(label = text_label),
    colour = "black",
    fill = "white",
    label.size = 0,
    size = 3,
    fontface = "bold",
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_linetype_manual(
    values = c(
      "Human" = "solid",
      "ChatGPT" = "dotted",
      "Gemini" = "dotdash",
      "Claude" = "longdash"
    )
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Human-Referenced x3 Trajectories",
    x = "Normalized sentence-pair position",
    y = "Predicted adjacent-sentence cosine similarity"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  ggplot2::theme(
    panel.grid = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(colour = "black"),
    axis.title = ggplot2::element_text(colour = "black"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

modul_8_x3_human_reference_plot

ggplot2::ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    modul_8_output_dir,
    "modul_8_x3_human_reference_print_bw.png"
  ),
  plot = modul_8_x3_human_reference_plot,
  width = 10,
  height = 7,
  dpi = 300,
  bg = "white"
)
```

# Note: This cell fits a joint regression model of adjacent-sentence similarity over normalized text position, using the Human baseline as the reference category. It predicts the Human and LM trajectories on a common 0–1 scale and calculates each LM’s difference from Human at the beginning, middle, and end, as well as the mean absolute curve distance. The fitted trajectories, Human-relative summary, regression model, and combined comparison plot are saved for diagnostic analysis. (cf. \# MODUL 6.37d)

# # MODUL 8.10 — Plot the x3pcw paragraph-continuity means

```{r}

# MODUL 8.10 — Plot the x3pcw paragraph-continuity means

modul_8_x3pcw_plot_data <- modul_8_x3pcw_profile |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    text_label = factor(
      text_label,
      levels = text_label[order(text_order)]
    )
  )

modul_8_x3pcw_histogram <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  modul_8_x3pcw_plot_data,
  ggplot2::aes(
    x = text_label,
    y = x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean
  )
) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(
    fill = "#E5E5E5",
    colour = "#000000",
    width = 0.6,
    linewidth = 0.6
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_text(
    ggplot2::aes(
      label = sprintf(
        "%.3f",
        x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean
      )
    ),
    vjust = -0.5,
    colour = "#000000",
    size = 3.5,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Paragraph-continuity profiles (x3pcw) across texts",
    subtitle = "Mean adjacent-paragraph similarity comparison",
    x = "Text source",
    y = "Mean adjacent-paragraph cosine similarity (x3pcw)"
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    expand = ggplot2::expansion(
      mult = c(0, 0.15)
    )
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  ggplot2::theme(
    panel.grid = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(
      colour = "#000000"
    ),
    axis.title = ggplot2::element_text(
      colour = "#000000"
    ),
    plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(
      colour = "#000000",
      face = "bold"
    ),
    plot.subtitle = ggplot2::element_text(
      colour = "#000000"
    ),
    panel.border = ggplot2::element_rect(
      colour = "#000000",
      linewidth = 0.8
    )
  )

ggplot2::ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    modul_8_output_dir,
    "modul_8_x3pcw_continuity_chart.png"
  ),
  plot = modul_8_x3pcw_histogram,
  width = 7,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 300,
  bg = "white"
)

modul_8_x3pcw_histogram
```

# MODUL 8.11 — Display human-centered connector continuity

This cell reloads ... + Similarity of an ordered LM connector transition to the fixed ordered Human connector-transition reference. This accounts for both: 1. the connector-semantic content of the two sentences; 2. their sequential roles as preceding sentence and following sentence.
The resulting vector has 100 dimensions, but no new LSA model is fitted. It simply preserves the order of two existing 50-dimensional vectors. This is preferable to the summed-vector construction because the sum loses direction. For MODUL 6.37i, I recommend the ordered concatenation construction.

```{r}
# MODUL 8.11 — Display Human-centered connector continuity

# MODUL 8.11 — Display Human-referenced connector divergence

x3cc_path <- file.path(
  project_root,
  "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x3co_connector_continuity.rds"
)

stopifnot(file.exists(x3cc_path))

x3cc_bundle <- readRDS(x3cc_path)

stopifnot(
  is.data.frame(x3cc_bundle$summary),
  all(
    c(
      "text_id",
      "model",
      "connector_type",
      "x3co_similarity_mean"
    ) %in% names(x3cc_bundle$summary)
  )
)

x3cc_summary <- x3cc_bundle$summary |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    model = dplyr::recode(
      as.character(model),
      human = "Human",
      human_baseline = "Human",
      chatgpt_native = "ChatGPT",
      claude_native = "Claude",
      gemini_native = "Gemini"
    ),
    connector_type = factor(
      connector_type,
      levels = c("CCONJ", "SCONJ")
    )
  )

x3cc_human_baseline <- x3cc_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(model == "Human") |>
  dplyr::select(
    connector_type,
    human_connector_similarity_mean = x3co_similarity_mean
  )

stopifnot(
  nrow(x3cc_human_baseline) == 2L,
  !anyDuplicated(x3cc_human_baseline$connector_type)
)

modul_8_x3cc_divergence <- x3cc_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(model != "Human") |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    x3cc_human_baseline,
    by = "connector_type"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    signed_divergence_from_human =
      x3co_similarity_mean -
      human_connector_similarity_mean,

    absolute_divergence_from_human =
      abs(signed_divergence_from_human),

    model = factor(
      model,
      levels = c("ChatGPT", "Claude", "Gemini")
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(
    model,
    connector_type
  )

stopifnot(
  nrow(modul_8_x3cc_divergence) == 6L,
  !anyNA(
    modul_8_x3cc_divergence[
      c(
        "model",
        "connector_type",
        "human_connector_similarity_mean"
      )
    ]
  )
)

modul_8_x3cc_table <- modul_8_x3cc_divergence |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Model = model,
    `Connector type` = as.character(connector_type),
    `LM mean connector-only cosine similarity` =
      x3co_similarity_mean,
    `Fixed Human mean connector-only cosine similarity` =
      human_connector_similarity_mean,
    `Signed divergence from Human mean` =
      signed_divergence_from_human,
    `Absolute divergence from Human mean` =
      absolute_divergence_from_human
  ) |>
  gt::gt(
    groupname_col = "Connector type"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md(
      "**Human-referenced connector divergence**"
    ),
    subtitle = gt::md(
      paste(
        "Text-level mean connector-only cosine similarity;",
        "fixed Human baseline calculated separately for",
        "CCONJ and SCONJ"
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(
      `LM mean connector-only cosine similarity`,
      `Fixed Human mean connector-only cosine similarity`,
      `Signed divergence from Human mean`,
      `Absolute divergence from Human mean`
    ),
    decimals = 3,
    use_seps = FALSE
  ) |>
  gt::sub_missing(
    columns = gt::everything(),
    missing_text = "NA"
  ) |>
  gt::cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = gt::everything()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = gt::md(
      paste(
        "**Exact measures:** LM and Human values are",
        "text-level means of connector-only adjacent-sentence",
        "cosine similarities. Signed divergence is",
        "LM mean minus the fixed Human mean for the same",
        "connector type. Absolute divergence is the absolute",
        "value of that difference."
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(
      color = "black"
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_title(
        groups = c("title", "subtitle")
      ),
      gt::cells_row_groups(),
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_body(),
      gt::cells_source_notes()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(
      weight = "bold"
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_row_groups(),
      gt::cells_column_labels()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "all",
      color = "black",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_row_groups(),
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_body()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.width = gt::pct(100),
    table.font.size = gt::px(11),
    heading.title.font.size = gt::px(14),
    heading.subtitle.font.size = gt::px(11),
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    row_group.background.color = "white",
    source_notes.background.color = "white",
    row.striping.include_table_body = FALSE,
    table.border.top.style = "solid",
    table.border.top.width = gt::px(1),
    table.border.top.color = "black",
    table.border.bottom.style = "solid",
    table.border.bottom.width = gt::px(1),
    table.border.bottom.color = "black"
  )

modul_8_x3cc_table

```


An approximative interpretation scale would look like:

Cosine value	Interpretation
close to 1	very strong connector continuity
moderately positive	partially similar connector organisation
close to 0	little connector-based similarity
moderately negative	contrasting connector organisation
close to −1	strongly opposed latent connector profiles

-> A positive value means that the connector-only vectors of two adjacent sentences point in a similar latent-semantic direction. A value close to zero means that the connector patterns of the two sentences have little systematic similarity. A negative value means that the two connector-only vectors point in opposing directions in the latent space.
->  A very high value indicates stability or repetition in connector organisation, but it cannot by itself establish that the discourse is more coherent, sophisticated, or grammatically successful. Likewise, a negative value may reflect a deliberate shift from one propositional relation to another.

# What x3cc measures
x3cc — text-internal connector continuity: cosine similarity between the connector-only vectors of two genuinely adjacent sentences within the same text.
Connector material: CCONJ and SCONJ are represented and evaluated separately.
Observation: one adjacent-sentence boundary, such as S1↔S2, for one connector type.
Valid pair: both adjacent sentences must contain a valid vector for the same connector type.
Mean connector continuity: the mean of all valid adjacent-sentence connector cosines within one text and connector type.
Pair coverage: the proportion of all adjacent-sentence boundaries for which a valid connector cosine could be calculated.
Analytical status: a supporting diagnostic within the x3 semantic-continuity indices, not a replacement for the sentence-, paragraph-, or content-word-based core measures.
Role of the frozen x3cc_bundle:The central analytical object is continuity. The other elements preserve its construction, aggregation, audit trail, and visual reuse.

# Note: Decoupling of Semantics and Propositional Structure (NEEDS REVISION)

The indices’ x3co plotting (Plot x) suggested that, within a very similar topic and semantic space, the logico‑semantic structure of the discourse is organised relatively differently. As realised through the patterning of connectors, this appears as a connector‑only latent structural skeleton versus a “content mass”. If content‑word similarity shows monotonic decay (e.g., thematic shifts as sentences progress), but connector continuity does not, it means that the logical architecture of the text is decoupled from the topical progression we can observe within the human baseline. So even when the human writer and the models are writing or providing output about basically the same thing, the way they thread that content with coordinating and subordinating relations is not coupled to the semantic content in the same way as in the human texts. 

# 

```{r}
# MODUL 8.11a — Display the existing MODUL 6 connector-continuity plot

modul_6_x3cc_plot_path <- file.path(
  measure_output_dir,
  "modul_6_connector_continuity.png"
)

stopifnot(file.exists(modul_6_x3cc_plot_path))

knitr::include_graphics(
  modul_6_x3cc_plot_path
)
```
# MODUL 8.11b — Display exact connector inventory

```{r}

readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x3co_connector_continuity.rds"
))$sentence_segments |>
  dplyr::filter(n_connectors > 0) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    lemma = stringr::str_split(stringr::str_squish(terms), "\\s+"),
    Text = dplyr::recode(as.character(model),
      human = "Human", human_baseline = "Human",
      chatgpt_native = "ChatGPT", gemini_native = "Gemini",
      claude_native = "Claude")
  ) |>
  tidyr::unnest_longer(lemma) |>
  dplyr::count(connector_type, lemma, Text) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = Text, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  dplyr::select(connector_type, lemma, Human, ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) |>
  gt::gt(groupname_col = "connector_type") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**Exact connector inventory by target text**"),
    subtitle = "CCONJ and SCONJ lemma occurrence counts"
  ) |>
  gt::cols_label(lemma = "Connector lemma") |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_row_groups())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_row_groups(), gt::cells_body())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    row_group.background.color = "white"
  )
```

# MODUL 8.11c - Same but with bigger contrast for CCONJ-only display

```{r}
# 1. Daten filtern und für den 3-Panel-Vergleich vorbereiten
cconj_data <- x3cc_plot |>
  dplyr::filter(tolower(connector_type) == "cconj") |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    model = factor(model, levels = c("ChatGPT", "Gemini", "Claude"))
  )

# 2. Erzeugen UND das Drucken ERZWINGEN (über print)
print(
  ggplot2::ggplot(
    cconj_data,
    ggplot2::aes(
      x = normalized_position,
      y = connector_continuity,
      group = source,
      linetype = source,
      shape = source,
      fill = source
    )
  ) +
    ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.3, colour = "grey50") +
    ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
    ggplot2::geom_point(colour = "black", size = 2.5, stroke = 0.6) +
    
    # Trennung in exakt 3 nebeneinanderliegende Diagramme
    ggplot2::facet_wrap(~model, nrow = 1) +
    ggplot2::coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-1, 1)) +
    
    # Schwarz-Weiß-Zuweisung
    ggplot2::scale_shape_manual(name = "Source", values = c("Human" = 21, "LM" = 24)) + 
    ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(name = "Source", values = c("Human" = "#000000", "LM" = "#FFFFFF")) + 
    ggplot2::scale_linetype_manual(name = "Source", values = c("Human" = "solid", "LM" = "dashed")) +
    
    # Design
    ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 10) + 
    ggplot2::theme(
      legend.position = "bottom",
      panel.grid = ggplot2::element_blank(),
      axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(colour = "black"),
      strip.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = "white", colour = "black", linewidth = 0.7),
      strip.text = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold")
    ) +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = "CCONJ Connector Continuity: Model Divergence from Human Baseline",
      x = "Normalized sentence-transition position",
      y = "Adjacent-sentence connector cosine similarity"
    )
)

# 3. Datei sichern
ggplot2::ggsave(
  filename = file.path(project_root, "measure_outputs", "modul_6_cconj_baseline_comparison.png"),
  width = 9,
  height = 4,
  dpi = 300,
  bg = "white"
)
```

#

```{r}

```


# Note: x3cw

x3cw is the original construction name for: content-word-only adjacent-paragraph semantic continuity.
It uses paragraph vectors containing only: NOUN, VERB, ADJ, ADV.
Each paragraph is projected into the frozen 50-dimensional actual-LSA reference space. 
Cosine similarity is then calculated between genuinely adjacent paragraphs:P1↔P2,P2↔P3,P3↔P 4,…
The original measure name was: x3cw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity.

This object: x3cw_projection contains the actual-LSA projections of the content-word paragraph segments.
Its central component is: x3cw_projection$vectors.Each row is one paragraph vector in the frozen 50-dimensional actual-LSA space. Its role is to preserve the numerical representation used to calculate cosine similarity. It does not itself contain the adjacent-paragraph results.

This helper function  retrieves two paragraph vectors and calculates:sum(vector_1 * vector_2)
Because the projected vectors were L2-normalized, their dot product equals cosine similarity.
The function returns NA when either vector contains missing values. It does not replace invalid comparisons with zero.
This x3cw_paragraph_pairs is the pair-level analytical table. The original similarity column was: x3cw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity, later on it was renamed  to: x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity.
There are 11 adjacent paragraph pairs across the four target texts.
normalized_position

The position of each paragraph transition within its text was normalized and the object was named: normalized_position
pi = i−1/ n−1. The first paragraph boundary receives 0 (= the first transition receives 0), and the final boundary receives 1 (= the final transition receives 1. ).
Where:
i is the ordered transition number;
n is the number of adjacent transitions in that text; 
For x3pcw: x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity ~ normalized_position he regression is then:si=β0 +β1pi+εi
For the gradient model, the function retains only paragraph-transition rows with finite normalized positions and finite x3pcw cosine values. It does not modify or delete rows from the frozen pair-level table.

In the completed x3pcw calculation, all 11 pair-level x3pcw values were finite. Therefore, no substantive paragraph transition was actually lost from the final gradient analysis.

Its role is to make trajectories from texts with different numbers of paragraphs comparable on the same beginning-to-end scale. It is not a similarity measure or the positional predictor used for the gradient. However,the positional predictor for the gradients is normalized_position. It is not an x3 measure itself, but the ordered 0–1 predictor used to estimate how an x3 semantic-continuity measure changes from the beginning to the end of a text.
For both x3s and x3pcw, the positional predictor is: normalized_position.


The original text-level summary table is in the object called: x3cw_profiles; and its purpose was to deliver the later renamed:x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean. This mean answers:
How high or low is content-word-based adjacent-paragraph semantic continuity across the text?

x3 semantic-continuity indices
- x3s is sentence-level adjacent semantic continuity (x3s_adjacent_sentence_similarity).
- x3p

x3p is adjacent-paragraph semantic continuity using the complete seven-POS target layer:

cw: NOUN, PROPN, VERB,ADJ, ADV; co: CCONJ, SCONJ. x3p remains conceptually distinct from x3pcw because x3p includes connectors and proper nouns, whereas x3pcw restricts the paragraph representation to four content-word POS categories.

For the forthcoming y4 comparison, x3p is not the matched measure.

This function calculate_x3pcw_gradient() fits one separate linear model within each text:
x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity ~ normalized_position

n_valid_x3pcw_gradient_pairs - this is the number of valid adjacent-paragraph transitions available for estimating the gradient. It is essential for interpretation because, ChatGPT has three transitions;Human has four transitions; Claude and Gemini have only two transitions. Claude’s and Gemini’s slopes are therefore two-point differences rather than sustained trajectories. x3pcw_topic_continuity_first - is the observed x3pcw cosine at the first paragraph boundary. It represents the starting level of content-word paragraph continuity. 
x3pcw_topic_continuity_change - this is final similarity − first similarity. It expresses the direct observed change between the first and final paragraph transitions. It is related to, but not identical with, the regression gradient.

x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_gradient is the estimated regression slope over normalized text position. It answers:
Does content-word paragraph continuity tend to increase, decrease, or remain comparatively stable from the beginning to the end of the text?
Interpretation:
positive: increasing tendency;
near zero: little net linear direction;
negative: declining tendency. (x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_gradient_se is the standard error of the estimated slope. It is retained only where at least three transitions are available. It remains NA for Claude and Gemini.) 

x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_gradient_r2 - is the proportion of variation in the observed paragraph-continuity values represented by the fitted straight line. It does not measure the strength of continuity itself.
Interpretation:
higher R2 : the linear trajectory represents the observed points relatively well;
low R2: the observed trajectory fluctuates and the straight line is a weak abstraction;
NA: insufficient observations for a meaningful diagnostic.

MODUL 5 y4 indice for comparison: y4_content_jaccard, measures exact lexical overlap between adjacent paragraphs.
For each identical adjacent paragraph boundary, it compares the sets of content lemmas using the Jaccard coefficient:

J(A,B)= ∣A∩B∣/∣A∪B∣ where A and B are the content-lemma sets of two adjacent paragraphs. It uses the same four content-word categories as x3pcw:NOUN, VERB, ADJ, ADV.
A high y4 value means that many exact content lemmas recur across the paragraph boundary. It does not capture synonymy or semantic similarity between different words. 
The final comparison is: y4_content_jaccard ↔ x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity
Both measures:
use the same 11 adjacent paragraph transitions;
- operate at paragraph level;
- use NOUN, VERB, ADJ, and ADV;
- preserve one observation per identical paragraph boundary.
Their meanings differ:
   y4_content_jaccard → exact recurrence of content lemmas
   x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity → latent semantic continuity between content-word paragraph vectors
This is therefore a comparison between lexical recurrence and semantic continuity

* Representation level and observation level overview
| Dimension            | Question                                                      |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Representation level | What linguistic unit was vectorized?                          |
| Observation unit     | What is one measured data point?                              |
| Aggregation level    | At what level is the reported result summarised?              |
| Analytical function  | Is it a level, variability, direction, or diagnostic measure? |

* Micro (Local(Clauses, PoS, mwCombos)/Sentence),Meso (Paragraph-level) and Macro-level (overall text) classification

| Analytical unit                      | Representation / observation level                      |   |
| ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------- | - |
| Sentence vector                      | Local sentence level within its boundaries              |   |
| Adjacent sentence pair               | Local transition level                                  |   |
| Paragraph vector                     | Meso-level within p and its sect boundaries             |   |
| Adjacent paragraph pair              | Meso-level transition level                             |   |
| Text-level mean, SD, gradient, (R^2) | Macro-level summary of local or meso-level observations |   |
Micro: local clauses, POS, mwCombos, and sentences; Meso: paragraph level; Macro: overall text level.

* | Level of (chunk) representation      | Meaning                        |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| **s-level** | Sentence-level representation or observation              |
| **p-level** | Paragraph-level representation or observation             |
| **t-level** | Whole-text-level representation or aggregated observation |

Importante: The level on which an index is constructed must be distinguished from the level at which its resulting observation is reported. A t-level result may be derived from ordered s-level or p-level observations.


* y4–x3pcw relationship analysis, which is a data-alignment object, because it ensures that each y4 value and each x3pcw value refer to the exact same paragraph boundary.
| Text  | Paragraph pair |                        y4 |                         x3pcw |
| ----- | -------------- | ------------------------: | ----------------------------: |
| Human | P1–P2          | lexical overlap for P1–P2 | semantic similarity for P1–P2 |
| Human | P2–P3          | lexical overlap for P2–P3 | semantic similarity for P2–P3 |

y4_x3pcw_matched_transitions role is to guarantee that lexical and semantic values are compared boundary by boundary rather than accidentally joining different transitions. (The object was frozen with 11 rows: seven LM transitions and four Human transitions, with no missing y4 or x3pcw values.)
The next question becomes: Across matched paragraph boundaries, is greater exact content-lemma recurrence associated with greater latent semantic continuity? The pooled coefficient combines two kinds of variation:
1. within-text variation
2. between-text variation
It may therefore be influenced by the fact that the Human transitions occupy a different region of the y4–x3pcw space from the LM transitions. 
What is the pooled coefficient? The pooled coefficient is the correlation or regression coefficient calculated using all 11 matched p-level transition observations together: 4 Human transitions + 7 LM transitions =11 pooled transitions
The pooled regression is:  x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity ~ y4_content_jaccard using all four texts in one model.
(pooled = Human and LM p-level transition observations are analysed together in one descriptive relationship)
Did we account for Human influencing the pooled result? Of course, that is exactly why three scopes were calculated:


| Scope      | p-level observations | Function                                              |
| ---------- | -------------------: | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| All texts  |                   11 | Pooled descriptive relationship                       |
| LMs only   |                    7 | Checks whether the relationship remains without Human |
| Human only |                    4 | Exploratory within-Human relationship                 |

* In the available observations, exact content-lemma overlap co-varies strongly with semantic continuity within the Human text, whereas this relationship is absent among the seven LM transitions. More texts per source would be required before presenting it as a real model-level contrast.

#  MODUL 8.12 — Display the x3pcw mean, change, and gradient profile

```{r}

# MODUL 8.12 — Display the x3pcw mean, change, and gradient profile


x3pcw_gradient_bundle <- readRDS(file.path(
  project_root,
  "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x3pcw_topic_continuity_gradient.rds"
))

x3pcw_profiles_extended <-
  x3pcw_gradient_bundle$profiles_extended

stopifnot(
  nrow(x3pcw_profiles_extended) == 4L,
  all(c(
    "text_id",
    "model",
    "x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean",
    "x3pcw_topic_continuity_first",
    "x3pcw_topic_continuity_final",
    "x3pcw_topic_continuity_change",
    "x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_gradient",
    "x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_gradient_se",
    "x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_gradient_r2",
    "n_valid_x3pcw_gradient_pairs"
  ) %in% names(x3pcw_profiles_extended))
)

modul_8_x3pcw_profile_table_data <-
  x3pcw_profiles_extended |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    model = dplyr::recode(
      as.character(model),
      human = "Human",
      human_baseline = "Human",
      chatgpt_native = "ChatGPT",
      gemini_native = "Gemini",
      claude_native = "Claude"
    ),
    model = factor(
      model,
      levels = c("Human", "ChatGPT", "Gemini", "Claude")
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(model) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Text = as.character(model),

    `Mean adjacent-paragraph cosine` =
      x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_mean,

    `First-transition cosine` =
      x3pcw_topic_continuity_first,

    `Final-transition cosine` =
      x3pcw_topic_continuity_final,

    `Final − first cosine` =
      x3pcw_topic_continuity_change,

    `LM slope: cosine ~ normalized position` =
      x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity_gradient,

    `Slope SE` =
      x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_gradient_se,

    `LM R²` =
      x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_gradient_r2,

    `Valid p-level transitions` =
      n_valid_x3pcw_gradient_pairs
  )

modul_8_x3pcw_profile_table <-
  modul_8_x3pcw_profile_table_data |>
  gt::gt(
    rowname_col = "Text"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md(
      "**x3pcw paragraph-level Topic-Continuity profile**"
    ),
    subtitle = gt::md(
      paste0(
        "Macro-level observations derived from meso-level ",
        "adjacent-paragraph cosine similarities"
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::cols_label(
    `Mean adjacent-paragraph cosine` =
      gt::md("Mean<br>cosine"),

    `First-transition cosine` =
      gt::md("First<br>cosine"),

    `Final-transition cosine` =
      gt::md("Final<br>cosine"),

    `Final − first cosine` =
      gt::md("Change<br>in cosine"),

    `LM slope: cosine ~ normalized position` =
      gt::md("Linear-model<br>slope"),

    `Slope SE` =
      gt::md("Slope<br>SE"),

    `LM R²` =
      gt::md("Linear-model<br>R²"),

    `Valid p-level transitions` =
      gt::md("Valid p-level<br>transitions")
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(
      `Mean adjacent-paragraph cosine`,
      `First-transition cosine`,
      `Final-transition cosine`,
      `Final − first cosine`,
      `LM slope: cosine ~ normalized position`,
      `Slope SE`,
      `LM R²`
    ),
    decimals = 3,
    use_seps = FALSE
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(
    columns = `Valid p-level transitions`
  ) |>
  gt::sub_missing(
    columns = gt::everything(),
    missing_text = "NA"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = gt::md(
      paste0(
        "1 Measurement level: x3pcw uses meso-level content-word ",
        "paragraph vectors and adjacent-paragraph transition observations. ",
        "The displayed mean, change, gradient, SE, and R² are macro-level."
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = gt::md(
      paste0(
        "2 Measure: adjacent-paragraph cosine similarity; the gradient is the estimated change in cosine similarity from normalized position 0 to 1."
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = gt::md(
      paste0(
        "3 For texts with only two valid paragraph transitions, change and slope ",
        "are numerically equivalent because the positions are 0 and 1. ",
        "Slope SE and R² remain NA."
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(
      weight = "bold",
      color = "black"
    ),
    locations = gt::cells_column_labels(
      columns = gt::everything()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(
      weight = "bold",
      color = "black"
    ),
    locations = gt::cells_stub(
      rows = gt::everything()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "all",
      color = "black",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_column_labels(
        columns = gt::everything()
      ),
      gt::cells_stub(
        rows = gt::everything()
      ),
      gt::cells_body(
        columns = gt::everything(),
        rows = gt::everything()
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "top",
      color = "black",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = gt::cells_source_notes()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    stub.background.color = "white",
    data_row.padding = gt::px(4),
    table.font.color = "black",
    source_notes.font.size = gt::px(10)
  )

modul_8_x3pcw_profile_table
```
# Note: Measurement definition
| Column         | Exact measure                                                                              |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Mean           | Mean cosine similarity across adjacent content-word paragraph vectors                      |
| First          | Cosine similarity at the first adjacent-paragraph transition                               |
| Final          | Cosine similarity at the final adjacent-paragraph transition                               |
| Change         | Final cosine minus first cosine                                                            |
| Gradient       | Linear-regression slope of cosine similarity over normalized paragraph-transition position |
| Gradient SE    | Standard error of the linear-regression slope                                              |
| Gradient (R^2) | (R^2) of the regression of cosine similarity on normalized position                        |
| N              | Number of valid adjacent-paragraph transition observations                                 |

- The gradient model is: x3pcw cosine similarity=β0​+β1​(normalized paragraph-transition position)+ε
- In short: 
          + Mean cosine similarity
          + Linear-regression slope of cosine over normalized position
          + R² of that linear regression
          + Pearson correlation between y4 Jaccard and x3pcw cosine
For x3pcw:

Each cosine is a meso-level adjacent-paragraph transition observation. The mean summarizes all such meso-level cosine observations within one text and is therefore a macro-level text observation. The gradient uses their ordered sequence and is also a macro-level text observation.

#Technical Note for Modul 8.13 related to # MODUL 6.38b: 
This cell reads the two frozen pair-level sources, verifies identical paragraph boundaries, creates normalized within-text position, and freezes the 11-transition table. The y4 source already contains paragraph_number, next_paragraph_number, and y4_content_jaccard.
* We are doing this to test whether exact content-word repetition (y4) actually co-varies with latent paragraph-level Topic Continuity (x3pcw) across the same 11 paragraph transitions. The joined table is needed because the correlation, regression, and scatterplot require both measures aligned row by row for each identical transition.

# # MODUL 8.13 — Display the y4–x3pcw relationship diagnostics
This cell loads the frozen y4–x3pcw relationship diagnostics and displays the all-text, LM-only, and Human-only results. It does not rematch paragraph boundaries or recalculate Jaccard coefficients, cosine similarities, correlations, or regressions.

```{r}
# MODUL 8.13 — Display the y4–x3pcw relationship diagnostics

y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_path <- file.path(
  measure_output_dir,
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_y4_x3pcw_relationship_diagnostics.rds"
)

stopifnot(file.exists(y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_path))

y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_object <- readRDS(
  y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_path
)

required_diagnostic_columns <- c(
  "analysis_scope",
  "n_matched_transitions",
  "pearson_correlation",
  "spearman_correlation",
  "regression_intercept",
  "regression_slope",
  "regression_r2"
)

if (is.data.frame(y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_object)) {

  y4_x3pcw_diagnostics <-
    tibble::as_tibble(y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_object)

} else {

  diagnostic_tables <- y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_object |>
    purrr::keep(is.data.frame) |>
    purrr::keep(
      ~ all(
        required_diagnostic_columns %in% names(.x)
      )
    )

  stopifnot(length(diagnostic_tables) == 1L)

  y4_x3pcw_diagnostics <-
    tibble::as_tibble(diagnostic_tables[[1]])
}

stopifnot(
  nrow(y4_x3pcw_diagnostics) == 3L,
  all(
    required_diagnostic_columns %in%
      names(y4_x3pcw_diagnostics)
  )
)

modul_8_y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_data <-
  y4_x3pcw_diagnostics |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    analysis_scope = dplyr::case_when(
      analysis_scope %in%
        c("all_texts", "all texts", "All texts") ~
        "All texts",
      analysis_scope %in%
        c("lms_only", "lm_only", "LMs only") ~
        "LMs only",
      analysis_scope %in%
        c("human_only", "Human only") ~
        "Human only",
      TRUE ~ as.character(analysis_scope)
    ),
    analysis_scope = factor(
      analysis_scope,
      levels = c(
        "All texts",
        "LMs only",
        "Human only"
      )
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(analysis_scope) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Scope = as.character(analysis_scope),
    `Matched transitions` = n_matched_transitions,
    `Pearson r` = pearson_correlation,
    `Spearman ρ` = spearman_correlation,
    `Regression intercept` = regression_intercept,
    `Regression slope` = regression_slope,
    `Regression R²` = regression_r2
  )

modul_8_y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_gt <-
  modul_8_y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_data |>
  gt::gt(
    rowname_col = "Scope"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md(
      "**y4–x3pcw relationship diagnostics**"
    ),
    subtitle = gt::md(
      "Matched adjacent-paragraph transitions"
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Correlation",
    columns = c(
      `Pearson r`,
      `Spearman ρ`
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Linear regression",
    columns = c(
      `Regression intercept`,
      `Regression slope`,
      `Regression R²`
    )
  ) |>
  gt::cols_label(
    `Matched transitions` =
      gt::md("Matched<br>transitions"),
    `Pearson r` =
      gt::md("Pearson<br>r"),
    `Spearman ρ` =
      gt::md("Spearman<br>ρ"),
    `Regression intercept` =
      gt::md("Intercept"),
    `Regression slope` =
      gt::md("Slope"),
    `Regression R²` =
      gt::md("R²")
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(
    columns = `Matched transitions`,
    use_seps = FALSE
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(
      `Pearson r`,
      `Spearman ρ`,
      `Regression intercept`,
      `Regression slope`,
      `Regression R²`
    ),
    decimals = 3,
    use_seps = FALSE
  ) |>
  gt::sub_missing(
    columns = gt::everything(),
    missing_text = "NA"
  ) |>
  gt::cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = gt::everything()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = gt::md(
      paste0(
        "*Measure: relationship between y4 exact content-lemma ",
        "Jaccard overlap and x3pcw adjacent-paragraph cosine similarity; ",
        "the regression is `x3pcw cosine similarity ~ y4 Jaccard`."
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = gt::md(
      paste0(
        "Measurement level: meso-level adjacent-paragraph transitions."
      )
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(
      weight = "bold",
      color = "black"
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_column_spanners(),
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_stub()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "all",
      color = "black",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_column_spanners(),
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_stub(),
      gt::cells_body()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "top",
      color = "black",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = gt::cells_source_notes()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.width = gt::pct(100),
    table.font.size = gt::px(10),
    heading.title.font.size = gt::px(14),
    heading.subtitle.font.size = gt::px(10),
    source_notes.font.size = gt::px(10),
    data_row.padding = gt::px(8),
    column_labels.padding = gt::px(8),
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    stub.background.color = "white",
    source_notes.background.color = "white",
    table.font.color = "black"
  )

modul_8_y4_x3pcw_diagnostics_gt
```
Note: # Interpretative note:

The primary all-text analysis suggests 

greater exact content-lemma recurrence
tends to coincide with greater latent Topic Continuity, but only within the human baseline. The LM-only result is essentially zero:

Pearson r = −0.0224
Spearman ρ = −0.0714
R² = 0.0005

This means that exact content-word repetition does not meaningfully predict x3pcw Topic Continuity across the seven LM transitions!

The positive pooled relationship appears to be exclusivelly influenced by the four Human transitions, which show a strong positive pattern. Because the Human analysis has only four observations, it must remain exploratory.

Conclusive note: Across all eleven transitions, exact content-lemma recurrence showed a moderate positive descriptive association with latent Topic Continuity. This relationship disappeared when the analysis was restricted to the seven LM transitions, suggesting that the pooled association was substantially influenced by the Human paragraph transitions. Exact lexical recurrence and latent semantic continuity therefore cannot be treated as interchangeable properties in the present data.
The way the LMs use lexical material repetitively to maintain Topic Continuity does not lead to a corresponding Topic-Continuity resolution as measured with LSA. This is therefore one of the clearest decoupling indices the CCP has shown so far: exact lexical recurrence and latent semantic continuity are structurally distinct and do not necessarily co-vary.

More specifically, the LM-only analysis shows that the repetitive use of content lemmas does not contribute at all to explaining variation in x3pcw Topic Continuity:


r=-0.0224,\qquad
\rho=-0.0714,\qquad
R^2=0.0005.

Interpretation Note:  The LMs can therefore use lexical material repetitively to maintain the appearance of topic continuity without this repetition structurally leading to a corresponding latent Topic-Continuity resolution in the LSA space, something we know from Bajohr’s observations about “surface cohesion.” However, the CCP provides a more fine-grained hypothesis: in a deeper sense, as explained throughout the Theoretical Part, LMs are not even cohesive in a human-centered manner. Their cohesion is not motivated by meaning generation (which would converge on coherence), but by a task-driven notion of topicality. The result does not establish causality, but it provides strong descriptive evidence that lexical repetition cannot be treated as equivalent to semantic Topic Continuity.
# 14

```{r}
# MODUL 8.13 — Display frozen y4–x3pcw diagnostics

readRDS(file.path(
  project_root,
  "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_y4_x3pcw_relationship_diagnostics.rds"
)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    analysis_scope = dplyr::recode(
      analysis_scope,
      all_texts = "All texts",
      lms_only = "LMs only",
      human_only = "Human only"
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Scope = analysis_scope,
    `Matched transitions` = n_matched_transitions,
    `Pearson r` = pearson_correlation,
    `Spearman ρ` = spearman_correlation,
    Intercept = regression_intercept,
    Slope = regression_slope,
    `R²` = regression_r2
  ) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "Scope") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**y4–x3pcw relationship diagnostics**"),
    subtitle = "Content-word Jaccard overlap and adjacent-paragraph cosine similarity"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(columns = `Matched transitions`) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(`Pearson r`, `Spearman ρ`, Intercept, Slope, `R²`),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  gt::sub_missing(missing_text = "NA") |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold", color = "black"),
    locations = list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_borders(
      sides = "all",
      color = "black",
      weight = gt::px(1)
    ),
    locations = list(
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_stub(),
      gt::cells_body()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    stub.background.color = "white",
    table.font.color = "black"
  )
```
# Note: # Note: The Semantic Coherence Matrix
In the yet to follow matrix, Topic Continuity (operationalized as adjacent semantic similarity) is a graded measure of semantic continuity that can be higher or lower. The Topic-continuity gradient represents the change in adjacent semantic similarity over normalized text position (here on paragraph level, a paragraph was vectorized for the LSA to be applied). The x3 (x3s [sentnce-level], x3p [paragraph-level], x3pcw [content words over paragraphs]) indices were calculated over 
x3s sentence pairs:       102
x3s profiles:               4
+
x3p paragraph pairs:       11
x3p profiles:               4
+
x3pcw paragraph pairs:     11
x3pcw profiles:             4


Current x3pcw results
Mean Topic-Continuity level
Human      0.3056374
Claude     0.2454105
Gemini     0.1643951
ChatGPT    0.1482608

Ranking:

Human > Claude > Gemini > ChatGPT
Topic-continuity gradients
ChatGPT   +0.11153652
Claude    −0.13371286
Gemini    −0.27977701
Human     +0.05262017

Interpretation:

Human
→ highest overall Topic Continuity;
  small positive trajectory

Claude
→ relatively high Topic Continuity;
  declining trajectory

ChatGPT
→ lowest overall Topic Continuity;
  improving trajectory from a very low starting point

Gemini
→ low Topic Continuity;
  strongest decline

For texts with only two paragraph transitions, gradient standard error and R 2 remain NA.

Observed R
2
 values:

ChatGPT   0.1062
Human     0.0367
Claude    NA
Gemini    NA

The low values for ChatGPT and Human show that their trajectories are not strongly linear.

Semantic Coherence Matrix
Topic-continuity level	Positive or near-stable gradient	Negative gradient
High	Human: high and comparatively stable continuity	Claude: high level, fading continuity
Low	ChatGPT: low but improving continuity	Gemini: low level, rapid continuity loss

Out of need for normalization, we hypothesize a idealized Point d’Or which configaration would be

high Topic-Continuity level + gradient near zero = high and stable semantic continuity

The human generated text stands out in that regard and is currently closest to this configuration.

Topic Continuity (Level): This is given by the mean of adjacent semantic similarity. It answers the question: How high or low is the topic continuity throughout the text over existent paragraphs?

Trajectory (Gradient): This is given by the change in adjacent semantic similarity over normalized text (continuiation). It answers the question: Does the topic continuity increase, decrease, or remain stable as the text progresses?

Hovewer it appears obvious to ask whether and how is paragraph-level LSA Topic Continuity associated with exact content-lemma overlap? Because current text-level rankings appear different: y4 content-lemma overlap → 1.ChatGPT > 2. Human > 3.Gemini > 4. Claude and x3pcw Topic Continuity → 1. Human > 2. Claude > 3. Gemini > 4. ChatGPT,  the analysis asks whether higher exact content-lemma overlap predicts higher LSA-based Topic Continuity, which motivates the the later pair-level correlation analysis. Therefore to grasp that central research question at this point the relevant measures become  y4 (content_jaccard) and x3pcw and its exploring their relation:

y4_content_jaccard ↔ x3pcw_adjacent_paragraph_similarity

Both measures use the same 11 adjacent paragraph transitions, i.e. they use NOUN, VERB, ADJ, ADV and operate on paragraph level.

y4_content_jaccard shows the exact recurrence of content lemmas, while x3pcw is intended to latent semantic Topic Continuity. 


# MODUL 8.15 — Display frozen y4–x3pcw continuity-waves plot

```{r}
# MODUL 8.15 — Display frozen y4–x3pcw continuity-waves plot

knitr::include_graphics(file.path(
  project_root,
  "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_combined_continuity_waves.png"
))
```

# MODUL 8.15 — Display frozen internal_consistency_proxy indices

```{r}
# MODUL 8.15 — Display frozen internal_consistency_proxy indices

readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x4_title_subheading.rds"
))$profiles |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    model = dplyr::recode(as.character(model),
      human = "Human", human_baseline = "Human",
      chatgpt_native = "ChatGPT", gemini_native = "Gemini",
      claude_native = "Claude")
  ) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Text = model,
    `Sentence–title cosine mean` = x4_sentence_title_similarity_mean,
    `Paragraph–title cosine mean` = x4_paragraph_title_similarity_mean,
    `Whole-text–title cosine` = x4_text_title_similarity,
    `Paragraph–subheading cosine mean` =
      x4_paragraph_subheading_similarity_mean
  ) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "Text") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**internal_consistency_proxy indices**"),
    subtitle = "Title- and subheading-based cosine similarities"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(columns = gt::everything(), decimals = 3) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
```
