```{r}
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub(), gt::cells_body())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    stub.background.color = "white"
  )
```

# Note: Sentence → title mean
Each body sentence was compared with the shared main-title vector using cosine similarity. The sentence similarities were then averaged separately for each text. Paragraph → title mean Each original body paragraph was compared with the same main-title vector. The paragraph similarities were then averaged separately for each text. Whole text → title The complete body text was represented by one LSA vector and compared once with the main-title vector. Therefore, this is a single cosine similarity, not a mean of sentence or paragraph values.

* Note: Main new measure - x4_paragraph_subheading_similarity_mean -\> For each text:

merged Section 1 was compared with Subheading 1; merged Section 2 was compared with Subheading 2; merged Section 3 was compared with Subheading 3; the three cosine similarities were averaged equally.

Section 1 → Subheading 1 = s1 Section 2 → Subheading 2 = s2 Section 3 → Subheading 3 = s3

Each comparison contributes exactly one third to the final value:=\> s1+s2+s3/3

Caveat note:The paragraph-to-subheading similarity should be interpreted cautiously because the subheadings may have been added after the body text had already been written.

(In that case, the measure captures the retrospective semantic fit between each section and its assigned subheading, rather than necessarily showing whether the text was originally developed in response to/toward that subheading.) \#
* # Note: These numbers say, how well each model and the human baseline “sticks to its own headings” at different structural levels. Interpreting the columns as LSA‑based semantic similarity between each unit and its title (higher = more on‑topic):

Whole text → title: All four have the highest values here (0.26–0.46), so at the macro level every text broadly fits its overall title. The human text is strongest (0.4571), followed by Claude, then ChatGPT, with Gemini lowest.

Paragraph → title/ subtitle: Again, the human text is at or near the top (0.3191 paragraph, 0.0317 subheading; note that the subheading value is small for everyone), with Claude and ChatGPT close behind and Gemini consistently lower. This suggests that human paragraphs and sections are more tightly semantically aligned with their titles than the LM ones, especially compared to Gemini.

Sentence → title: At the micro level, ChatGPT (0.1551) and the human text (0.1375) are quite close, with Claude slightly lower and Gemini lowest. That is, individual human and ChatGPT sentences are more semantically related to the text title than Claude’s or Gemini’s are.

Put simply: across all structural levels, the human text shows the strongest and most consistent semantic anchoring to its titles, Claude is generally next, ChatGPT slightly behind but still reasonably aligned, and Gemini is weakest, especially at the paragraph, whole‑text, and sentence levels. This supports the view that humans keep their discourse more tightly organised around the announced topics, while Gemini didn't manage to stick to the prompted headings.

# MODUL 8.16 — Display frozen seed-word diagnostics

```{r}
# MODUL 8.16 — Display frozen seed-word diagnostics

readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x4_expected_seed_word_diagnostics.rds"
))$diagnostics |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Text = model,
    Expected = n_sw_expected,
    Detected = n_sw_detected,
    Annotated = n_sw_annotated,
    `In model` = n_sw_in_model,
    OOV = n_sw_oov
  ) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "Text") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**Seed-word diagnostics**"),
    subtitle = "Exact counts from the frozen x5_prompt_compliance output"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(columns = gt::everything()) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub(), gt::cells_body())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    stub.background.color = "white"
  )
```
# Note:  Gemini was intentionally excluded because of the Markdown-related annotation corruption.
# Meaning of the MODUL 6.40 diagnostics

n_sw_expected: all 61 seed-word occurrences prescribed by the prompt. n_sw_detected: prescribed seed-word occurrences actually found in the text. n_sw_annotated: detected occurrences with both lemma and POS annotation. n_sw_in_model: detected and annotated occurrences with an eligible POS whose lemma exists in the actual-LSA vocabulary. These exact rows can form the seed vector. n_sw_oov: detected and LSA-relevant occurrences whose lemmas are outside the actual-LSA vocabulary. \# Note: n_sw_detected is used within MODUL 6.40 as an exploratory x4-associated diagnostic of expected seed-word presence. The retained values are 48/61 for ChatGPT and 50/61 for Claude. Gemini is excluded because retained Markdown emphasis markers systematically corrupted parts of its token and POS annotation. The Human baseline is excluded from the comparative diagnostic because it is the source of the expected seed-word list and supplies the Human-derived seed component for MODUL 6.41. n_sw_detected remains separate from x4_text_prompt_induced_ass_net_similarity and must not be averaged with or added to the cosine score calculated in MODUL 6.42. During final integration, values are available only for ChatGPT and Claude; Gemini and Human should remain missing for this diagnostic, with their respective exclusion reasons documented. \# Because n_sw_detected equals n_sw_annotated for both models, there is no annotation failure among the detected words. Instead:

ChatGPT omitted 13 required seed-word occurrences. Claude omitted 11 required seed-word occurrences. Of the detected words, only 25 and 27 respectively can enter the actual-LSA vector because the remaining detected words have excluded POS categories or are OOV. 

# 

```{r}
# MODUL 8.17 — Display frozen x5_prompt_compliance indices
# MODUL 8.17 — Display frozen x5_prompt_compliance similarities

x5 <- readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x5_prompt_compliance_bundle.rds"
))

dplyr::left_join(
  tibble::tibble(
    Comparison = c("ChatGPT–Human", "Claude–Human", "ChatGPT–Claude"),
    `Full associative-net cosine` = c(
      x5$chatgpt_human_assoc_net_similarity,
      x5$claude_human_assoc_net_similarity,
      x5$chatgpt_claude_assoc_net_similarity
    )
  ),
  dplyr::rename(
    x5$seed_word_net_similarities,
    Comparison = comparison,
    `Seed-word-net cosine` = seed_word_net_similarity
  ),
  by = "Comparison"
) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "Comparison") |>
  gt::tab_header(title = gt::md("**x5_prompt_compliance similarities**")) |>
  gt::fmt_number(columns = gt::everything(), decimals = 3) |>
  gt::tab_style(gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub())) |>
  gt::tab_style(gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
                list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub(), gt::cells_body())) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
  source_note = paste0(
    "Cosine scale: −1 = opposite direction, 0 = no directional similarity, ",
    "1 = identical direction. Values are interpreted comparatively within ",
    "the same frozen 50-dimensional actual-LSA space."
  )
) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    stub.background.color = "white"
  )

```
# Note: 
The seed-word list was fixed at 61 expected seed words for every text. However, not every LM implemented all 61 seed words, resulting in detected seed-word subsets of 48 for ChatGPT and 50 for Claude. In addition, only seed words belonging to the selected LSA categories—NOUN, PROPN, VERB, ADJ, ADV, CCONJ, and SCONJ—could enter the LSA projection, while many function words were excluded by design. The remaining seed words also had to be represented in the frozen actual-LSA vocabulary. Consequently, the final Seed-word nets are based on 35 seed words in model for Human, 25 for ChatGPT, and 27 for Claude. This reduction is therefore mainly due to incomplete LM implementation, POS-based LSA selection, and OOV cases, rather than an additional annotation loss.


# 

```{r}
# MODUL 8.18 — Display frozen x6_txts_lsa_sim indices

readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_6_actual_lsa_x6_txts_lsa_sim_bundle.rds"
))$whole_text_similarities |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Comparison = comparison,
    `Whole-text cosine similarity` = whole_text_similarity
  ) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "Comparison") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**x6_txts_lsa_sim indices**"),
    subtitle = "Pairwise cosine similarity between complete body-text vectors"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(columns = gt::everything(), decimals = 3) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
    source_note = paste0(
      "Cosine similarity ranges from −1 to 1. Higher values indicate more ",
      "similar semantic direction within the same frozen 50-dimensional ",
      "actual-LSA space. Values are interpreted comparatively; no universal ",
      "cut-offs apply."
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_stub(), gt::cells_body())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_source_note(
  source_note = paste0(
    "Cosine scale: −1 = opposite direction, 0 = no directional similarity, ",
    "1 = identical direction. Values are interpreted comparatively within ",
    "the same frozen 50-dimensional actual-LSA space."
  )
) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    stub.background.color = "white"
  )
```
# Note: 
LM–Human comparisons show how closely each LM text aligns semantically with the Human text.LM–LM comparisons show how closely the LM texts align with one another.
Thus, the LM texts are more similar to one another than to Human. The cosine measures semantic direction.

# # MODUL 8.19 — Render the frozen x2 distributional-plausibility architecture

```{r}
# MODUL 8.19 — Render the frozen x2 distributional-plausibility architecture

DiagrammeR::grViz("
digraph G {
  graph [layout=dot, rankdir=LR, splines=true, overlap=false]
  node [fontname='Helvetica', shape=box, style='filled', fillcolor='#F4F6F7',
        color='#BDC3C7', fontcolor='#2C3E50']
  edge [color='#7F8C8D', arrowhead=vex, arrowsize=0.8]

  root [label='x2_distributional_plausibility', fillcolor='#2C3E50',
        fontcolor='white', style='bold,filled', shape=folder]

  x2a [label='x2a\\nfunctor-based frames', fillcolor='#3498DB', fontcolor='white']
  x2b [label='x2b\\n+2_sliding_cxw_PMI', fillcolor='#E67E22', fontcolor='white']
  x2c [label='x2c\\nconnector sequence PMI', fillcolor='#2ECC71', fontcolor='white']

  x2a1 [label='x2a1_nominal\\npre_f ↔ functor']
  x2a2 [label='x2a2_predicative\\nfunctor ↔ post_f']
  x2c1 [label='x2c1_subc_sw\\nSCONJ sequence']
  x2c2 [label='x2c2_cor_sw\\nCCONJ sequence']

  x2a_txt [label='Root-region frames in retained body sentences\\nwith VERB/AUX root',
           shape=note, fillcolor='#FFF9C4']
  x2a1_txt [label='First parser-linked preverbal\\nPRON, NOUN, or PROPN candidate',
            shape=note, fillcolor='#FFF9C4']
  x2a2_txt [label='Parser-identified obj/iobj nominal head',
            shape=note, fillcolor='#FFF9C4']
  x2b_txt [label='Ordered q0+q1+q2 windows\\nwith final q0+q1 residual',
           shape=note, fillcolor='#FFF9C4']
  x2c1_txt [label='Sequence beginning with SCONJ',
            shape=note, fillcolor='#FFF9C4']
  x2c2_txt [label='Sequence beginning with CCONJ',
            shape=note, fillcolor='#FFF9C4']

  root -> x2a
  root -> x2b
  root -> x2c

  x2a -> x2a_txt [style=dotted]
  x2a -> x2a1
  x2a -> x2a2
  x2a1 -> x2a1_txt [style=dotted]
  x2a2 -> x2a2_txt [style=dotted]

  x2b -> x2b_txt [style=dotted]

  x2c -> x2c1
  x2c -> x2c2
  x2c1 -> x2c1_txt [style=dotted]
  x2c2 -> x2c2_txt [style=dotted]
}
")
```
# Note: MODUL 7.2 — Analytical scope

MODUL 7 evaluates the corpus-based statistical (distributional) plausibility of approved (present in corpus) multiword combinations/sequences extracted from the Human, ChatGPT, and Claude texts. `BASIC_GEMINI_NATIVE_001` is excluded because its Markdown contamination produced systematically unreliable token, POS, and dependency annotation. The aim was to gain insight into the overall MI (PMI) structure of each target text. 

The application of the "functor" (unsaturated elements, that license semantic slots and call for their closure, as the conceptualized middle of a functor-argument relations is taken from Reinöhl & Ellison 2024 (795,797,800) (Reinöhl, Uta, and T. Mark Ellison. 2024. “Metaphor Forces Argument Overtness.” Linguistics 62(4): 795–847. https://doi.org/10.1515/ling-2021-0072). 

The x2a indices bundle represents parser-restricted functor-formulated frames that have root status. `x2a1_nominal` pairs the verbal functor with an approved preverbal nominal candidate, while `x2a2_predicative` pairs it with a parser-identified nominal object. The x2b and x2c  indices bundles represent exact ordered two- or three-token lemma sequences - that are motivated by the sentence lengths and sstructure (type of connector). Titles and subheadings are excluded.

(P)MI was obtained from the fixed DeReKo reference corpus using token-based queries; inflectional variants were therefore not grouped under shared lemmas. The values represent the corpus association of the queried token pair or exact two- or three-token sequence and were thus derived at the clause-level. The distributional, statistical plausibility of the whole text was subsequently reconstructed by averaging these clause-level (P)MI values.

Procedure:
In MODUL 7, the distributional plausibility of the word combinations occurring in the three target texts is examined on the basis of a human-authored reference corpus. (BASIC_GEMINI_NATIVE_001 is excluded from MODUL 7 because Markdown-related contamination produced systematically unreliable token, POS, and dependency annotation. Gemini is retained only in the exclusion audit and does not enter candidate extraction, corpus querying, PMI calculation, aggregation, or final reporting.) For this purpose, all candidates are first preserved with their token and lemma forms as well as their unambiguous assignment to text_id, sentence, and original token position. In simple main clauses and in the respective root area of more complex sentences, the areas pre_f = nominal candidates, functor = VERB, and post_f = predicative candidates are distinguished for x2a1 and x2a2: In the sentence “Menschen entwickeln neue Fähigkeiten,” for example, Menschen ↔ entwickeln can be examined as a connection between pre_f = nominal candidates and functor, and entwickeln ↔ Fähigkeiten as a connection between functor and post_f = predicative candidates. Subordinate clauses and coordinated continuations, by contrast, are not analysed again through this functor logic, but are divided from a recognized SCONJ or CCONJ onwards into directly consecutive sliding windows: From “weil Menschen neue Fähigkeiten entwickeln,” for example, the lemma windows weil mensch neu, mensch neu fähigkeit, neu fähigkeit entwickeln, and finally fähigkeit entwickeln are created. Titles and subheadings are fully excluded. In the subsequent DeReKo pilot, lemma and token queries are tested in parallel; the lemma level will probably serve as the primary PMI query, while the token level is preserved as a control, diagnostic, or fallback representation. \# Caveat: It must be explicitly noted that a lemma-based query groups different inflectional forms under one lemma; therefore, the concrete inflectional structure of the word combination realized in the target text cannot be tested for plausibility.

* Note about the Corpus: Institut für Deutsche Sprache (2026): Deutsches Referenzkorpus DeReKo / Archiv der Korpora geschriebener Gegenwartssprache. Mannheim: Leibniz-Institut für Deutsche Sprache. Online verfügbar unter: https://ids-mannheim.de.

Für das DeReKo-Korpus:Kupietz, M., Belica, H., Keibel, H., & Witt, A. (2010). The German Reference Corpus DeReKo: A primordial corpus for linguistic research. In Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC 2010) (pp. 1848–1854). Valletta, Malta: European Language Resources Association (ELRA) \[1.2\].Für die KorAP-Infrastruktur:Bański, P., Diewald, N., Hanl, M., Kupietz, M., & Witt, A. (2014). Accessing the German Reference Corpus DeReKo via KorAP. In Proceedings of the 14th Conference of the European Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics (EACL 2014) — Software Demonstrations (pp. 5–8). Gothenburg, Sweden: Association for Computational Linguistics \[1.2\].Für das R-Package:Kupietz, M., Margaretha, E., & Diewald, N. (2020). RKorAPClient: An R package for accessing the German Reference Corpus DeReKo via KorAP. In Proceedings of the 12th International Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC 2020) (pp. 7017–7023). Marseille, France: European Language Resources Association (ELRA) \[1.2\].

# Technical Note: MODUL 7.10 reconnects the 747 globally deduplicated DeReKo queries with the original 752 occurrences in the three target texts.

Its importance lies in the fact that the query cache contains only one result per unique query, while the analysis requires text-level attribution. MODUL 7.10 therefore:

-   maps each corpus frequency and PMI value back to its original text occurrence;
-   preserves candidate_type, window size, sentence, and window_id_control;
-   reconstructs text-internal multiword combinations;
-   calculates n_mw_Combo and occurrence-weighted PMI diagnostics;
-   creates the analytical basis for all final text-level PMI summaries and reporting tables.

Without MODUL 7.10, the corpus results would remain detached from the texts and could not be interpreted as text-level distributional-plausibility measures.

# Note: The following is still subject to revision

PMI values were derived from the Mannheim DeReKo reference corpus, reflecting corpus-based distributional association. DeReKo is the world’s largest collection of German-language corpora and an empirical basis for linguistic research. The Mannheim German Reference Corpus and the Corpora of Contemporary Written German at the IDS together constitute the world’s largest linguistically motivated collection, with over 63.8 billion words as of January 2026, and they can be accessed free of charge via COSMAS II and KorAP. (cf. https://www.ids-mannheim.de/en/digspra/pb-s1/projects/corpus-development/). Human-baseline distance has not yet been calculated.

1.000 = everything that could be scored was scored.

Below 1.000 = only part of the eligible material produced a valid PMI value.

| Column           | Meaning                        | Format     |
|------------------|--------------------------------|------------|
| `text_id`        | Text origin                    | text       |
| `candidate_type` | x2 branch                      | text       |
| `token_n_in_w`   | 2-token or 3-token calculation | integer    |
| `n_total`        | approved queried combinations  | integer    |
| `n_ok`           | combinations with valid PMI    | integer    |
| `pmi_coverage`   | `n_ok / n_total`               | 3 decimals |
| `mean_pmi`       | mean valid PMI in bits         | 3 decimals |
| `median_pmi`     | robust central PMI             | 3 decimals |

-   

-   ChatGPT’s six approved functor–predicate combinations had a mean PMI of 3.569, compared with 0.816 across eleven Human combinations, producing an observed difference of 2.753. However, this difference was based on only six ChatGPT and eleven Human combinations and is therefore interpreted as an exploratory text-specific pattern rather than a general model effect. Therefore, more texts per source would be required before presenting it as a real model-level contrast.

-   Claude generated relatively few connector-based x2c windows. This pattern should be inspected alongside its y6 function-word and connector proportions: agreement would support comparatively lower connector density, whereas divergence would suggest that the reduced x2c count is mainly attributable to text length, clause length, or the restricted connector-window extraction rules. PMI is a base-2 logarithmic association measure, reported in bits.

PMI Interpretation under independence 0 observed as often as expected by chance 1 2 times the expected rate 2 4 times the expected rate 3 8 times the expected rate −1 half the expected rate 8 256 times the expected rate

A positive PMI therefore indicates that the words or sequence occur together more often than expected from their individual corpus frequencies. There is no fixed upper bound. That is why very rare combinations can produce very high PMI.

An unattested combination does not receive PMI 0. Its mathematical PMI would be undefined because the joint probability is zero. Frequency and PMI answer different questions:

Quantity Question corpus_frequency How often did the combination occur? corpus_pmi How unexpectedly strong is the combination relative to the frequencies of its individual words?

A frequent combination can have low PMI when its words are individually very frequent. A rare combination can have high PMI when its component words are also rare. However, a frequency of one does not automatically produce an odd PMI. The value also depends on the marginal frequencies and corpus total. Nevertheless, singleton and very-low-frequency combinations are exactly where PMI estimates are least stable.

-   What does frequency_only mean? In this implementation, frequency_only means that the combination had a returned joint corpus frequency, but one or more quantities required for PMI were unavailable or invalid. For exact MWS combinations, valid PMI requires the frequency of the full combination, each component’s marginal frequency, and a valid common corpus/event-space total. If the full combination frequency exists but the complete PMI denominator cannot be constructed, the row receives query_status = frequency_only, corpus_frequency = returned value, and corpus_pmi = NA. frequency_only does not correct rare-event bias; it merely prevents an invalid PMI from being calculated. It belongs in the technical audit, not in the final association table.

What does unattested mean? query_status = unattested, corpus_frequency = 0, and corpus_pmi = NA. The exact pair or sequence was not found in the selected DeReKo virtual corpus. PMI is not set to zero because zero would mean “observed at exactly the expected rate.” With zero observed co-occurrences, PMI is mathematically undefined in the ordinary unsmoothed formulation. What exactly is an mwCombo? Term meaning in this project: 1. mwCombo An extracted pair or exact multiword-window candidate, 2. MWS A sequence containing two or more words with variying associative strength, 3. collocation A sequence whose components show sufficient statistical contingency (cf. Yi 2021) 4 formulaicity Broader degree of conventionalization or entrenchment; PMI captures only one statistical dimension. Consequently, the pipeline asks:

To what degree does each extracted mwCombo display corpus-based contingency?

High PMI indicates that its components combine more frequently than predicted from their marginal frequencies. Low PMI indicates weak association. An unattested combo has no estimable unsmoothed PMI.

# MODUL 8.20 — Display frozen MODUL 7 indices-bundles overview

```{r}
# MODUL 8.20 — Display frozen MODUL 7 indices-bundles overview

readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_7_pmi_final_summary.rds"
)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Text = dplyr::recode(
      text_id,
      HUMAN_BASELINE_001 = "Human",
      BASIC_CHATGPT_NATIVE_001 = "ChatGPT",
      BASIC_CLAUDE_NATIVE_001 = "Claude"
    ),
    `Indices bundle` = dplyr::case_when(
      candidate_type %in% c("x2a1_nominal", "x2a2_predicative") ~
        "x2a functor-based",
      candidate_type == "x2b" ~
        "x2b +2_sliding_cxw_PMI",
      candidate_type %in% c("x2c1_subc_sw", "x2c2_cor_sw") ~
        "x2c connector sliding-window PMI"
    ),
    Index = dplyr::recode(
      candidate_type,
      x2a1_nominal = "x2a1 nominal–functor",
      x2a2_predicative = "x2a2 functor–object",
      x2b = "x2b sliding sequence",
      x2c1_subc_sw = "x2c1 SCONJ sequence",
      x2c2_cor_sw = "x2c2 CCONJ sequence"
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(
    factor(`Indices bundle`, levels = c(
      "x2a functor-based",
      "x2b +2_sliding_cxw_PMI",
      "x2c connector sliding-window PMI"
    )),
    factor(Index, levels = c(
      "x2a1 nominal–functor",
      "x2a2 functor–object",
      "x2b sliding sequence",
      "x2c1 SCONJ sequence",
      "x2c2 CCONJ sequence"
    )),
    factor(Text, levels = c("Human", "ChatGPT", "Claude")),
    token_n_in_w
  ) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    `Indices bundle`,
    Text,
    Index,
    `Tokens in query` = as.integer(token_n_in_w),
    `Valid PMI values` = as.integer(n_pmi),
    `Mean PMI` = as.integer(round(mean_pmi)),
    `Median PMI` = as.integer(round(median_pmi))
  ) |>
  gt::gt(groupname_col = "Indices bundle") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**x2 distributional-plausibility indices bundles**"),
    subtitle = "Whole-text summary of valid DeReKo PMI values"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(
    columns = c(
      `Tokens in query`, `Valid PMI values`,
      `Mean PMI`, `Median PMI`
    )
  ) |>
  gt::sub_missing(missing_text = "NA") |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_text(weight = "bold", color = "black"),
    list(
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_row_groups()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
    list(
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_row_groups(),
      gt::cells_body()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    row_group.background.color = "white",
    table.font.color = "black"
  )
```

# # Note: Pointwise mutual information measures the corpus-based contingency of a queried token combination relative to the occurrence expected from the marginal frequencies of its constituent tokens. A value of 0 indicates occurrence at the expected rate, whereas positive values indicate increasingly greater-than-expected association; because PMI is logarithmic and has no fixed upper bound, high values are mathematically possiblePMI3 values are not interpreted on the same numerical scale as PMI2. Comparisons are undertaken only within the same branch and token length; for example, x2b PMI3 across texts, never x2b PMI3 against x2b PMI2 or x2a PMI2. rare and singleton combinations can yield particularly high and less stable estimates. In accordance with the distinction between phrasal frequency and statistical contingency discussed by Yi (2021), PMI is interpreted here as an association measure rather than as raw recurrence or complete formulaicity.PMI2 and PMI3 quantify differently defined co-occurrences of mwCombos (multi word combinations): a two-token sequence and an exact three-token sequence, respectively. These mwCombos are token-based query units that retain the inflectional forms realized in the target texts and should not be equated with MWS, which tend to be more formulaic and are often analysed on a scale of lemmatization. Therefore, PMI2 and PMI3 values are not directly comparable. It should not be concluded that a PMI3 value of 8 indicates a stronger association than a PMI2 value of 4.

* n_pmi: how many valid PMI values entered the calculation.
mean_pmi: the arithmetic average of those PMI values.
median_pmi: the middle PMI value after sorting them.

** The individual PMI values are first obtained at the clause level for each approved mwCombo: x2a captures parser-based functor–nominal and functor–predicate relations in the main-clause/root region, whereas x2b and x2c capture exact two- or three-token windows, including connector-based combinations. These individual values are then averaged across the whole text separately for each index in the indices bundles, so the reported mean and median PMI are text-level summaries rather than single sentence- or window-level values. Comparisons are therefore made only within the same index and token length, while the indices bundles together reconstruct the text’s overall distributional plausibility from these clause-level associations.

**! The x2b index applies PMI to exact consecutive two- and three-token mwCombos extracted by a sliding-window procedure. The sliding window defines the local candidate units, while PMI quantifies the corpus-based contingency of each complete combination. This complements the parser-based x2a indices by capturing sequential associations that are not restricted to predefined functor relations.

# MODUL 8.21 — Display a reproducible random sample of 10 valid mwCombos

```{r}
# MODUL 8.21 — Display a reproducible random sample of 10 valid mwCombos

set.seed(20260721)

readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_7_x2_distributional_plausibility_bundle.rds"
))$mwCombo_by_text |>
  dplyr::filter(
    query_status == "ok",
    is.finite(corpus_pmi)
  ) |>
  dplyr::slice_sample(n = 10L) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Text = dplyr::recode(
      text_id,
      HUMAN_BASELINE_001 = "Human",
      BASIC_CHATGPT_NATIVE_001 = "ChatGPT",
      BASIC_CLAUDE_NATIVE_001 = "Claude"
    ),
    Index = dplyr::case_when(
      candidate_type == "x2a1_nominal" ~ "x2a1 nominal–functor",
      candidate_type == "x2a2_predicative" ~ "x2a2 functor–object",
      candidate_type == "x2b" ~ "x2b +2_sliding_cxw_PMI",
      candidate_type == "x2c1_subc_sw" ~ "x2c1 SCONJ sequence",
      candidate_type == "x2c2_cor_sw" ~ "x2c2 CCONJ sequence"
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Text,
    Index,
    `Tokens in query` = as.integer(token_n_in_w),
    mwCombo = pmi_window,
    `Corpus frequency` = as.integer(corpus_frequency),
    `Corpus PMI` = as.integer(round(corpus_pmi))
  ) |>
  gt::gt() |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**Random sample of valid mwCombos**"),
    subtitle = "Ten reproducibly sampled DeReKo query results"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(
    columns = c(`Tokens in query`, `Corpus frequency`, `Corpus PMI`)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_text(weight = "bold", color = "black"),
    gt::cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
    list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_body())
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    table.font.color = "black"
  )
```



# Note: the x2 distributional/statistical-plausibility drift profiles.
For each text, drift₀ describes its position relative to the human-authored DeReKo reference corpus. Corpus frequency and PMI are retained as separate components: mean corpus frequency indicates how frequently the queried combinations occur in DeReKo, while mean PMI indicates their association strength relative to the DeReKo marginals. The functor-based dimension is represented as functor-based PMI Σ, calculated from the separately averaged x2a1 and x2a2 values. For the sliding-window dimensions, only three-token PMI3 windows are included; final two-token residual windows are retained descriptively but excluded from the drift calculus.

driftₕ expresses the dimension-by-dimension difference between the Human reference text and each LM:

driftₕ,F(LM,j) = F̄(Human,j) − F̄(LM,j)

driftₕ,PMI(LM,j) = PMĪ(Human,j) − PMĪ(LM,j)

Consequently, a positive driftₕ indicates that the Human text has the higher value and that the LM falls below the Human reference in that dimension; zero indicates equality; and a negative value indicates that the LM exceeds the Human value. Frequency and PMI are not combined into a single scalar. The reported PMI2 exclusion percentage refers to the proportion of valid sliding-window mwCombo observations omitted from the drift calculus; because the windows overlap, it should not be interpreted as the percentage of unique body-text tokens removed.

# MODUL 8.22 — Display x2 distributional/statistical-plausibility drift₀ and driftₕ

```{r}
# MODUL 8.22 — Display x2 distributional/statistical-plausibility drift₀ and driftₕ

x2 <- readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_7_x2_distributional_plausibility_bundle.rds"
))$mwCombo_by_text |>
  dplyr::filter(query_status == "ok",
                is.finite(corpus_frequency), is.finite(corpus_pmi))

p <- x2 |>
  dplyr::filter(candidate_type %in% c("x2a1_nominal", "x2a2_predicative") |
                  token_n_in_w == 3L) |>
  dplyr::group_by(text_id, candidate_type) |>
  dplyr::summarise(F = mean(corpus_frequency), PMI = mean(corpus_pmi),
                   n = dplyr::n(), .groups = "drop")

profile <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  p |>
    dplyr::filter(candidate_type %in% c("x2a1_nominal", "x2a2_predicative")) |>
    dplyr::summarise(F = sum(F), PMI = sum(PMI), n = sum(n), .by = text_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Dimension = "functor-based PMI Σ"),
  p |>
    dplyr::filter(!candidate_type %in% c("x2a1_nominal", "x2a2_predicative")) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Dimension = paste0(candidate_type, " PMI3"))
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(Text = dplyr::recode(
    text_id, HUMAN_BASELINE_001 = "Human",
    BASIC_CHATGPT_NATIVE_001 = "ChatGPT",
    BASIC_CLAUDE_NATIVE_001 = "Claude"
  )) |>
  dplyr::select(Text, Dimension, F, PMI, n)

human <- profile |>
  dplyr::filter(Text == "Human") |>
  dplyr::select(Dimension, Human_F = F, Human_PMI = PMI, Human_n = n)

profile |>
  dplyr::filter(Text != "Human") |>
  dplyr::left_join(human, by = "Dimension") |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    LM = Text, Dimension,
    `Human mean F` = Human_F, `LM mean F` = F,
    `driftₕ,F` = Human_F - F,
    `Human mean PMI` = Human_PMI, `LM mean PMI` = PMI,
    `driftₕ,PMI` = Human_PMI - PMI,
    `Human n` = Human_n, `LM n` = n
  ) |>
  gt::gt(groupname_col = "LM") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**x2 distributional/statistical-plausibility drift₀ and driftₕ**"),
    subtitle = "driftₕ = Human − LM"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(columns = c(`Human mean F`, `LM mean F`, `driftₕ,F`), decimals = 2) |>   # 1. Alle Frequenz-Mittelwerte (F) als Dezimalzahl mit genau 2 Nachkommastellen (X.YY)
  gt::fmt_integer(columns = c(`Human mean PMI`, `LM mean PMI`, `driftₕ,PMI`, `Human n`, `LM n`)) |> # 2. PMI-Werte und n-Stichproben als saubere ganze Zahlen ohne Nachkommastellen (X) belassen
  gt::tab_style(gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_row_groups())) |>
  gt::tab_style(gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
                list(gt::cells_column_labels(), gt::cells_row_groups(),
                     gt::cells_body())) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    row_group.background.color = "white"
  )

x2 |>
  dplyr::filter(candidate_type %in% c("x2b", "x2c1_subc_sw", "x2c2_cor_sw")) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    `PMI2 excluded` = sum(token_n_in_w == 2L),
    `Valid sliding mwCombos` = dplyr::n(),
    `Excluded (%)` = 100 * `PMI2 excluded` / `Valid sliding mwCombos`,
    .by = text_id
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(Text = dplyr::recode(
    text_id, HUMAN_BASELINE_001 = "Human",
    BASIC_CHATGPT_NATIVE_001 = "ChatGPT",
    BASIC_CLAUDE_NATIVE_001 = "Claude"
  )) |>
  dplyr::select(Text, dplyr::everything(), -text_id) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "Text") |>
  gt::fmt_number(columns = `Excluded (%)`, decimals = 2) |>
  gt::tab_header(title = gt::md("**Sliding-window PMI2 exclusion**"))
```


# # MODUL 8.23 — Display sentence-length and neurolinguistical indices

```{r}
# MODUL 8.23 — Display sentence-length and neurolinguistical indices

sentence_length <- readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "module_independent_sentence_length_diagnostics.rds"
))

neurolinguistical <- readRDS(file.path(
  project_root, "measure_outputs",
  "modul_7_to_modul_8_neurolinguistical_diagnostics.rds"
))

dplyr::left_join(
  sentence_length,
  neurolinguistical,
  by = c("text_id", "text_label")
) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Text = text_label,
    `Body sentences` = n_body_sentences,
    `Mean sentence length` = sentence_length_words_mean,
    `Sentence-length SD` = sentence_length_words_sd,
    `Median sentence length` = sentence_length_words_median,
    `Word tokens` = n_word_tokens,
    `Word types` = n_word_types,
    Hapax = n_hapax,
    `Mean token length` = token_length_letters_mean,
    `Token-length SD` = token_length_letters_sd,
    TTR = ttr,
    `Brunet index` = brunet_index,
    `Honoré statistic` = honore_statistic,
    `Content density` = content_density,
    `Verb rate` = verb_rate,
    `Adjective rate` = adj_rate
  ) |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "Text") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = gt::md("**Module-independent neurolinguistical indices**"),
    subtitle = "Sentence-length, lexical-diversity, and lexical-density diagnostics"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Sentence length",
    columns = c(
      `Body sentences`,
      `Mean sentence length`,
      `Sentence-length SD`,
      `Median sentence length`
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Lexical inventory",
    columns = c(
      `Word tokens`, `Word types`, Hapax,
      `Mean token length`, `Token-length SD`
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Neurolinguistical indices",
    columns = c(
      TTR, `Brunet index`, `Honoré statistic`,
      `Content density`, `Verb rate`, `Adjective rate`
    )
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_integer(
    columns = c(
      `Body sentences`, `Word tokens`, `Word types`, Hapax
    )
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(
      `Mean sentence length`,
      `Sentence-length SD`,
      `Median sentence length`,
      `Mean token length`,
      `Token-length SD`,
      TTR,
      `Brunet index`,
      `Honoré statistic`,
      `Content density`,
      `Verb rate`,
      `Adjective rate`
    ),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  gt::sub_missing(missing_text = "NA") |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_text(weight = "bold", color = "black"),
    list(
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_column_spanners(),
      gt::cells_stub()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    gt::cell_borders(sides = "all", color = "black"),
    list(
      gt::cells_column_labels(),
      gt::cells_column_spanners(),
      gt::cells_stub(),
      gt::cells_body()
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_options(
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    stub.background.color = "white",
    table.font.color = "black"
  )
```

# Note: # ================ MODULE-INDEPENDENT Neurolinguistical_diagnostics realted Measures (inspired by Voleti, Liss and Berisha 2021)

Voleti, Rohit, Julie M. Liss, and Visar Berisha. 2021. “A Review of Automated Speech and Language Features for Assessment of Cognitive and Thought Disorders.” IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing 14(2): 282–298. https://doi.org/10.1109/jstsp.2019.2952087.
Measures like the following have proven inicative when it comes do diagnosis and classification of neurological thought disorder, as these "affect te ability of an individual to form complex thoughts and sentence structure, and might often have issues such as poverty of speech or disorganized speech(Voleti, Liss and Berisha 2021: 5). Therefore, the authors looked at methods that would allow insight into 'thought' content density, complexity of sentence syntax, semantic coherence, and sentiment analysis as research shows them to be relevant for these conditions. They distinguish between lexeme-level information, sentence-level informatin and finally semantics.

- Lexical diversity: measured by TTR and Brunét’s index.
- Lexical density: measured by content density (CD).
- verb_rate: proportion of verbs among all word tokens;
- adjective_rate: proportion of adjectives among all word tokens,
- n_hapax is a technical count required for Honoré’s statistic. Higher Honoré’s statistic generally indicates greater lexical diversity.

* # Note : TTR and Brunét’s index measure lexical diversity, that is, the degree of variation in the vocabulary used in a text. Content density measures the proportion of lexical content words—verbs, nouns, proper nouns, adjectives, and adverbs—among all word tokens. Lexical diversity and lexical density therefore describe different properties and must not be treated as interchangeable.

#

```{r}
1 + 1
```


# 

```{r}
1 + 1
```


# 

```{r}
1 + 1
```


# 

```{r}
1 + 1
```


# 

```{r}
1 + 1
```




```{r}
#| echo: false
2 * 2
```

The `echo: false` option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).
